name: Home
title: Home
description: Entry page that loads homepage content, highlights key boating safety and how-to resources, and routes users quickly to main sections and tools.

core_features: Load homepage content (intro text, featured beginner articles, featured tools, quick sections, and common task shortcuts) via getHomePageContent.
Provide a global search form in the header that sends the user to search_results.html with a query parameter.
Display quick section navigation cards based on quick_sections so users can jump to Safety Basics, How-To Guides, Checklists, Tools, Courses, and Glossary pages.
Display featured beginner Articles returned by getHomePageContent and route users to the appropriate article category page with identifying URL parameters.
Display featured Tools returned by getHomePageContent and route users to the Tools page with URL parameters indicating which tool to focus on.
Display common task shortcuts from common_tasks and route users to the relevant section pages with appropriate URL parameters to express task context.
Optionally focus or scroll to a target section on the page based on URL parameters, without using localStorage or other client-side persistence.
user_workflows: Start a site-wide search from Home: (1) User types a term into the header search input. (2) User submits the form. (3) The page navigates to search_results.html, appending ?query={encoded search term} to the URL. (4) The search results page interprets the query parameter.

Navigate to a main section using quick sections: (1) Home loads getHomePageContent and renders quick_sections. (2) User clicks a quick section card (e.g., safety basics, how-to guides, checklists, tools, courses, glossary). (3) The click handler maps the section key to the correct target page filename. (4) Browser navigates to that page, optionally including a section-specific URL parameter such as ?from=home or ?focusSection={key}.

Open a featured beginner article from Home: (1) Home loads featured_beginner_articles via getHomePageContent. (2) User clicks a featured article item. (3) The click handler selects the correct target category page (e.g., safety_basics.html or how_to_guides.html) based on the article.category. (4) Browser navigates to that page with a URL parameter such as ?articleId={article.id} or ?articleSlug={article.slug} so that the target page can highlight or scroll to the article.

Access a key tool or common task shortcut: (1) Home loads featured_tools and common_tasks via getHomePageContent. (2) User clicks a featured tool item or a common task shortcut. (3) The click handler maps the tool.slug or task_id to the appropriate destination (e.g., tools.html, checklists.html, courses.html, glossary.html, learning_path.html). (4) Browser navigates with URL parameters indicating which tool, checklist context, course level, or glossary focus to apply (for example, tools.html?toolSlug={tool.slug} or checklists.html?taskId={task_id}).
interactions: Header search submit: User enters text in the global search input and submits; event handler prevents default form submission, reads the input value, and navigates to search_results.html?query={encoded value}.

Quick section card click: User clicks on a quick_sections card; event handler reads the section key, maps it to the proper destination page (safety_basics.html, how_to_guides.html, checklists.html, tools.html, courses.html, glossary.html, or learning_path.html) and performs a navigation, optionally including context parameters like ?focusSection={key}.

Featured article click: User clicks on a featured beginner article item; event handler reads the Article properties (such as id, slug, and category) and navigates to the appropriate category page with URL parameters (e.g., safety_basics.html?articleId={id}) so the target page can resolve which article was chosen.

Featured tool click: User clicks on a featured tool item; event handler reads Tool.id and Tool.slug, then navigates to tools.html with URL parameters (e.g., tools.html?toolSlug={slug} or tools.html?toolId={id}) so the tools page can bring that tool into focus.

Common task click: User selects a common task shortcut; event handler reads task_id and uses a routing rule to determine the corresponding destination page (checklists.html, tools.html, courses.html, glossary.html, or learning_path.html), then navigates with a task-specific URL parameter such as ?taskId={task_id} so the destination page can interpret the task context.

Optional section focus on load: When Home loads, it inspects the current URL for optional parameters like focusSection or scrollTo. If present, after getHomePageContent resolves and content is rendered, the page scrolls to or visually focuses the corresponding section (e.g., quick sections, featured articles, common tasks).
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page is served at index.html with no required URL parameters. On each page load, the page invokes getHomePageContent once to retrieve hero_title, hero_subtitle, intro_text, featured_beginner_articles (Article[]), featured_tools (Tool[]), quick_sections, and common_tasks. The resulting data fully drives the content of the page; no static data is used. The page does not store or read state from localStorage or any other client-side persistence; all state that needs to survive navigation is represented via URL parameters. The header search form, when submitted, navigates the browser to search_results.html?query={user query}. Clicks on quick section cards navigate to the relevant section pages (safety_basics.html, how_to_guides.html, checklists.html, tools.html, courses.html, glossary.html, learning_path.html) and may append parameters such as ?from=index or ?focusSection={section key} for contextual behavior on the destination page. Featured article clicks navigate to the relevant category page and append a parameter like ?articleId={article.id} or ?articleSlug={article.slug} so the destination can highlight that article. Featured tool clicks navigate to tools.html with a parameter like ?toolSlug={tool.slug} or ?toolId={tool.id}. Common task clicks navigate to the appropriate destination page using a routing rule based on task_id, appending ?taskId={task_id} and, if needed, additional parameters (for example, indicating a target boat_size_category, difficulty level, or specific tool slug). Optionally, Home can interpret inbound parameters such as focusSection={key} or scrollTo={component-id} to automatically scroll to a given component after content is loaded, but it never triggers additional interfaces beyond getHomePageContent.

components.functionality: On initial page load, call getHomePageContent, wait for the response, and distribute hero_title, hero_subtitle, intro_text, featured_beginner_articles, featured_tools, quick_sections, and common_tasks to child components via props or a shared state mechanism.

Render the main textual introduction for the Home page using hero_title, hero_subtitle, and intro_text from getHomePageContent. It does not perform any navigation itself but provides a concise explanation of the site's purpose and what users can do from this page.

Provide a single text input and submit button for site-wide search in the global header. When submitted, it reads the current input value and navigates the user to search_results.html with the query URL parameter set to that value.

Use quick_sections from getHomePageContent to render a set of navigation items (cards or links) for the main site areas (Safety Basics, How-To Guides, Checklists, Tools, Courses, Glossary, and optionally a shortcut to My Learning Path). Each item uses the section key and description to decide its target URL.

Display the featured_beginner_articles returned by getHomePageContent as a list of clickable article items with title and summary metadata. It does not load article content itself; instead it routes the user to the appropriate article category page with identifiers encoded in the URL.

Display featured_tools from getHomePageContent as a set of clickable shortcuts to important tools, such as calculators or schedulers. When a tool is selected, the component navigates the user to tools.html and includes URL parameters derived from the Tool entity (e.g., slug or id) so the tools page can focus on the selected tool.

Render the common_tasks array from getHomePageContent as a list of practical task shortcuts (each with a title and description) that route users directly into the most relevant section for completing that task. The component interprets task_id according to a predefined routing rule to determine the correct destination page and parameters.

After home-data-loader has finished and components are rendered, read optional URL parameters such as focusSection or scrollTo. If present, locate the corresponding component or DOM anchor (for example, quick-sections-grid, featured-beginner-articles-list, or common-tasks-panel) and scroll or focus it, without triggering any additional API calls.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered max-width container (approx. 1100–1200px). Main content column spans roughly 8–9 columns and the sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the classic blog pattern from the mockup: a shallow horizontal header at the very top, then a centered max‑width content container that splits into a dominant left main column and a narrower right sidebar. Within the main column, content flows top‑to‑bottom in this order: page header block (title/intro), a full‑width featured tools strip, a slim section focus controller bar, a quick sections navigation grid, and then a vertical list of featured beginner articles laid out as media‑object rows. The right sidebar starts near the top of the content area and stacks utility content (common tasks panel) top‑to‑bottom. All components follow a linear vertical rhythm inside their respective columns.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the classic blog pattern from the mockup: a shallow horizontal header at the very top, then a centered max‑width content container that splits into a dominant left main column and a narrower right sidebar. Within the main column, content flows top‑to‑bottom in this order: page header block (title/intro), a full‑width featured tools strip, a slim section focus controller bar, a quick sections navigation grid, and then a vertical list of featured beginner articles laid out as media‑object rows. The right sidebar starts near the top of the content area and stacks utility content (common tasks panel) top‑to‑bottom. All components follow a linear vertical rhythm inside their respective columns.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies about 10–15% of the initial viewport height. Below it, the main container uses a 12‑column grid, with the left content column taking roughly 8–9 columns (~70%) and the right sidebar 3–4 columns (~30%). The featured tools strip and section focus controller span the full 12‑column container width. Inside the article list, each row assigns ~30–35% of the row width to the thumbnail image (left) and ~65–70% to text/CTA (right). Vertical spacing between major blocks (page header, tools strip, section focus, quick sections, article list, sidebar widgets) is around 32–48px, while internal padding within cards and panels is 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate and editorial. Each component gets generous breathing room: the page header block and featured tools strip have ample white space above and below; the quick sections grid uses comfortable gaps between tiles; the article list shows one clear card‑like row per article with a single CTA; and the common tasks panel in the sidebar uses a large, readable tap area for each shortcut. Visual clutter is avoided by limiting each block to a single primary purpose and avoiding multiple competing CTAs within the same row.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow retains an F‑pattern. Users first register the centered logo and the left/right header controls (MENU on the left, search on the right via the header search form). Their gaze then drops slightly to the left‑aligned page title in the page header block. From there, the eye follows the left column downward: first scanning the full‑width featured tools strip, then the section focus controller bar, then exploring the quick sections grid, and finally moving through the list of beginner articles left‑to‑right (image to title to excerpt to CTA). After scanning a few article rows, the eye naturally shifts to the right sidebar, anchored by the strong label bar at the top of the common tasks panel, then proceeds downward through any additional shortcut items. The section focus controller, placed just above the two‑column area, provides a subtle, persistent orientation to what part of the site the user is browsing.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a clean, centered blog‑style home with a white background and a quiet, editorial feel. A shallow header spans the full width at the very top, with a simple horizontal bar: MENU text at the far left, a compact boat‑themed logo centered, and the search control on the far right. Below this, within a max‑width container of about 1100–1200px, a page header block introduces the home page with a left‑aligned title and a short line of copy, surrounded by ample top and bottom breathing room. Immediately beneath, a full‑width featured tools strip stretches across the container as a low, horizontal band of tool shortcuts. Under that, a slim section focus controller bar sits, visually tying tools and content together. The layout then resolves into a classic two‑column structure: the left 70% of the width hosts the main content, starting with a quick sections grid of large, tappable tiles, followed by a vertical list of beginner‑friendly articles, each presented as a row with a thumbnail on the left and descriptive text plus a bright marine‑blue button on the right. The right 30% forms a sidebar that begins at roughly the same vertical height as the quick sections grid and contains a card‑like common tasks panel with a dark label strip and neatly stacked shortcuts. Generous vertical spacing between each band and card, along with consistent typography and a restrained marine‑blue accent, creates a calm, legible home page that guides users from orientation, to tools, to navigation, and finally into core learning content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader behaves as a full‑page overlay that sits above the entire content area but below the browser’s own chrome. When active, it covers the main container (from just beneath the site header down to the bottom edge of the viewport) with a semi‑transparent white veil, dimming the underlying two‑column layout without shifting it. A simple, centered loading indicator appears both horizontally and vertically within the container width, aligned to the visual center of the viewport. No other elements move; the loader simply masks interactions until initial data for the home page content is ready.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlay layer positioned fixed relative to the viewport; stretches full width of the page and full viewport height below the header; normally hidden and only displayed during data loading states.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page header block sits immediately below the global header bar, inside the centered max‑width container. It spans the full 12‑column width of the container, with left and right padding of about 24px. The main title is left‑aligned, positioned roughly one base spacing step (32–48px) below the bottom of the header bar, and a short descriptive line sits directly beneath it. The block’s total height is modest—roughly 96–144px—but the generous vertical whitespace above and below the title ensures it feels airy and editorial rather than cramped. On large screens, the text occupies roughly the left half of the container, leaving the right half as clean white space, maintaining the minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block anchored at the top of the content area, expanding naturally with text; does not stick or float when scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header search form is placed within the top header bar on the far right side, mirroring the mockup’s search icon placement. Horizontally, the bar is divided into three conceptual zones: MENU label on the left, centered logo, and the search control on the right. The search form occupies roughly 20–25% of the header’s width but is visually compact: on wide screens it presents as a subtle search icon or a short input field aligned to the right edge with about 24px right padding to the viewport edge. Vertically, it is centered within the shallow header band, maintaining equal top and bottom padding (approximately 12–16px) so it aligns with the logo and MENU label. When expanded (if designed as an expanding field), it overlays or extends leftwards into the header bar while remaining confined to that band’s height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in place within the global header region; scrolls away with the header if the header is not sticky. The field itself may expand or collapse in place but does not affect the layout flow of the content below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick sections grid resides in the left main column, below the section focus controller and above the featured beginner articles list. It starts roughly 32–40px beneath the section focus bar and aligns with the left edge of the main column (about 24px inset from the container edge). On desktop, it forms a 2‑column grid of navigation tiles: each tile occupies about 50% of the main column width, with 16–24px horizontal gutter between the two columns and 24–32px vertical gaps between rows. Each tile is a flat card with internal padding of about 16–24px, a clear section label, and possibly a small icon, all left‑aligned. The grid’s total width is constrained to the main column (about 70% of the container), and its vertical height depends on the number of tiles, but overall it feels like a compact, scannable launchpad sitting just above the article list. On narrower screens it would naturally collapse to a single column, but on desktop it keeps the two‑column structure for an efficient yet airy look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column; it pushes the article list downward but does not overlap or stick. It resizes responsively with the main column width while keeping consistent gutters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured beginner articles list occupies the majority of the left column, starting approximately 32–48px below the quick sections grid. It spans the full width of the main column (8–9 grid columns), with 24px horizontal padding inside the column edge. Each article is presented as a horizontal media‑object row: on each row, a rectangular thumbnail image sits on the left, taking about 30–35% of the row’s width, while the right‑hand text block fills the remaining 65–70%. The row height is governed by the image height and the length of the excerpt, typically around 180–240px. Within the right text block, the article title appears in bold at the top, followed by a short 2–3 line excerpt, then a single bright marine‑blue “continue” style CTA button aligned to the left, with around 8–16px of space between text and button. Vertical spacing between article rows is generous—around 32–40px—ensuring a clear separation and comfortable scannability as users scroll down the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list that extends downward in the main column and determines much of the page’s scroll length. Rows expand vertically according to content but maintain the fixed left‑image/right‑text structure at larger breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured tools strip sits directly beneath the page header block and above the section focus controller, spanning the entire width of the central content container (all 12 columns). It appears as a low horizontal band with a subtle background tint to differentiate it from the white page, but with flat styling and no heavy shadows. Inside, a horizontal row of tool items is aligned left, each occupying roughly equal width segments—typically 3–4 tools visible across on desktop. Each tool card has internal padding of about 16px and may include a small icon, short label, and a minimal one‑line description. Spacing between tool cards is around 16–24px. The strip has about 24–32px of vertical padding above and below its content, making it visually important but not overpowering. It reads as a quick‑access band that users see immediately after the page title before diving into deeper content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full‑width band within the main content flow; it pushes the section focus controller and the two‑column layout downward but does not stick or overlay adjacent content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The common tasks panel is positioned at the top of the right sidebar, starting roughly in line with the vertical position of the quick sections grid in the left column. It occupies the full width of the sidebar (3–4 columns, about 30% of the container width), inset by about 24px from the container’s right edge. The panel resembles the mockup’s author card: a dark marine‑blue or muted dark label bar sits at the very top, spanning the panel’s width and containing a concise title like “COMMON TASKS” in uppercase. Below the bar, the body of the panel is a white card with 16–24px padding on all sides. Inside, a vertical list of task shortcuts is stacked with consistent spacing (12–16px between items), each shortcut using clear, left‑aligned text and possibly a small leading icon. The panel’s top and bottom edges have around 24–32px margin to separate it from any other sidebar widgets, giving it a strong, self‑contained presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the sidebar column by default. Optionally, it can become sticky when the user scrolls past its original position, remaining pinned within the visible portion of the sidebar while respecting a small top offset (e.g., 16–24px) below the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section focus controller is a slim horizontal bar placed between the featured tools strip and the start of the two‑column layout. It spans the full width of the content container (all 12 columns) but keeps its interactive elements clustered toward the left, aligned with the left main column edge. Vertically, it sits about 24–32px below the bottom of the tools strip and about 24px above the quick sections grid and the top of the sidebar, acting as a visual hinge between tools and content. The controller might use a segmented control or pill‑shaped buttons (e.g., “Beginners,” “Trip Planning,” “On the Water”), laid out horizontally with 8–16px gaps, all in a subtle gray or outlined style, while the active segment uses the bright marine‑blue fill. The bar itself is visually light, with minimal height (roughly 40–48px including padding), preserving the minimalist feel while giving users a clear way to switch which topical slice of content is emphasized below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in the normal document flow, but can become sticky at the top of the content container once scrolled (just below the global header), so that the focus options remain visible as users scroll through the articles and sidebar. When sticky, it stretches full container width and maintains its left alignment with the main column.

name: Safety Basics
title: Safety Basics Articles
description: Page to browse, filter, sort, and favorite boating safety basics articles using dynamic filters and URL-based state.

core_features: Load available difficulty, rating, read-time, and sort options via getSafetyBasicsFilterOptions and render them as interactive filter and sort controls.
Parse URL query parameters (difficulty, rating_min, read_time_max, sort, page) on load to initialize filter controls and current results page.
Request a paginated list of Safety Basics articles via listSafetyBasicsArticles using filters and sort derived from URL parameters, and display them as cards with metadata and favorite status.
Allow users to adjust filters (difficulty, minimum rating, maximum read time) and sorting, update the URL parameters accordingly, reset pagination when needed, and re-fetch the article list.

Enable users to add and remove articles from Favorites directly from article cards using addArticleToFavorites and removeArticleFromFavorites, updating the favorite state in the listing without page reload.
Support navigation to individual article details by clicking the article card title or a dedicated link, passing the article ID via article.html?id=ARTICLE_ID.
Provide pagination controls that update the page URL parameter and fetch the corresponding page of results without changing other filters.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Land on Safety Basics with pre-applied filters from another page
1. User clicks a navigation link (e.g., from index.html) that opens safety_basics.html with some URL parameters (such as difficulty, rating_min, read_time_max, sort).
2. On initial load, the page reads the URL parameters and stores them in the page’s in-memory state.
3. The page calls getSafetyBasicsFilterOptions with no parameters.
4. Once filter options are returned, the filter controls are rendered and initialized to reflect the values from the URL parameters (or defaults if parameters are missing).
5. The page builds a filters object from the URL (difficulty, rating_min, read_time_max) and a sort value, and calls listSafetyBasicsArticles with filters, sort, page, and page_size.
6. The user sees a list of article cards corresponding to the applied filters and sort.
7. If the user uses browser Back/Forward, the page re-parses the URL, updates filter controls and current page, and re-calls listSafetyBasicsArticles so the view always matches the URL.

Workflow: Refine articles by difficulty, rating, and read time
1. User adjusts the difficulty filter control (e.g., chooses another option from the difficulty list populated by getSafetyBasicsFilterOptions).
2. The difficulty change handler updates the in-memory filter state, sets the page parameter to 1, and updates the URL query parameters to reflect the new difficulty and page=1 (while preserving any existing rating_min, read_time_max, and sort parameters).
3. The page rebuilds the filters object from the updated state and calls listSafetyBasicsArticles.
4. The article list refreshes to show results matching the new difficulty.
5. User then adjusts rating_min and/or read_time_max via their controls.
6. Each change updates the URL parameters, resets page to 1, and triggers a new listSafetyBasicsArticles call.
7. The applied filters summary updates to reflect current filters based on URL/state.

Workflow: Sort and paginate results
1. User changes the sort control to another sort option returned by getSafetyBasicsFilterOptions.
2. The sort change handler updates the sort state and URL sort parameter, resets page to 1, and calls listSafetyBasicsArticles.
3. The article list re-renders in the new order.
4. User clicks a pagination control (e.g., next page or a specific page number).
5. The pagination handler updates the page state and the page URL parameter, leaving difficulty, rating_min, read_time_max, and sort unchanged.
6. The page calls listSafetyBasicsArticles with the new page value and the same filters and sort.
7. The new page of article cards is displayed.

Workflow: Favorite or unfavorite an article from the list
1. User finds an article in the list and clicks the Favorites toggle control on that article card.
2. If the article is currently not favorited (as indicated by the is_favorited flag from listSafetyBasicsArticles), the toggle handler calls addArticleToFavorites with the article’s ID.
3. On success, the handler updates the in-memory state for that article’s is_favorited flag and may use the returned total_favorites to update any global favorites indicator if present.
4. If the article is currently favorited, the toggle handler instead calls removeArticleFromFavorites with the article’s ID.
5. On success, the handler updates the in-memory state to mark is_favorited as false.
6. The article card instantly reflects the new favorite status without a full page reload.

Workflow: Open an article detail from the listing
1. User clicks on an article card’s title or dedicated "Read More" interaction.
2. The click handler reads the article’s ID from the article card’s bound data.
3. The handler navigates to article.html?id=ARTICLE_ID, leaving the Safety Basics page’s URL (with its filters and pagination) available in the browser history.
4. When the user clicks Back from article.html, the browser returns to safety_basics.html with the same URL parameters, and the page replays the standard load sequence (parse URL, call getSafetyBasicsFilterOptions, then listSafetyBasicsArticles) to restore the previous list view.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial page load
- Trigger: Page load event.
- Behavior: Parse current URL parameters (difficulty, rating_min, read_time_max, sort, page) into an internal state object. Call getSafetyBasicsFilterOptions to retrieve filter and sort configuration. After filter options load, initialize UI controls from URL/state. Build filters and sort objects, then call listSafetyBasicsArticles to retrieve and render the initial article list.

Interaction: Difficulty filter change
- Trigger: User changes the difficulty control selection.
- Behavior: Update the difficulty value in the internal filter state and URL query parameters (difficulty). Reset page to 1 in state and URL. Rebuild the filters object and call listSafetyBasicsArticles with updated filters, sort, page=1, and current page_size.

Interaction: Rating minimum filter change
- Trigger: User adjusts the minimum rating control (e.g., dropdown, radio, or similar).
- Behavior: Update rating_min in state and URL. Reset page to 1. Call listSafetyBasicsArticles with updated filters and current sort.

Interaction: Maximum read time change
- Trigger: User adjusts the max read time control (e.g., slider or numeric field) and commits the change (on release or blur).
- Behavior: Update read_time_max in state and URL. Reset page to 1. Call listSafetyBasicsArticles with updated filters and current sort.

Interaction: Clear a specific filter
- Trigger: User removes a filter via an applied-filter chip or clear button for that filter.
- Behavior: Remove the corresponding parameter from state and URL. Reset page to 1. Call listSafetyBasicsArticles with the remaining filters.

Interaction: Clear all filters
- Trigger: User clicks a "Clear filters" type control.
- Behavior: Reset difficulty, rating_min, and read_time_max in internal state and remove them from the URL query string. Reset page to 1. Preserve sort if required or reset sort to a default value as defined in state logic. Call listSafetyBasicsArticles with no filters (or default filters) and the chosen sort.

Interaction: Sort change
- Trigger: User selects a different sort option.
- Behavior: Update sort in state and URL. Reset page to 1. Call listSafetyBasicsArticles with current filters and new sort.

Interaction: Pagination
- Trigger: User clicks next/previous or a specific page number.
- Behavior: Update page in state and URL. Call listSafetyBasicsArticles with current filters and sort, and the new page value.

Interaction: Toggle favorite on an article card
- Trigger: User clicks the favorite/unfavorite control on an article card.
- Behavior: Determine current favorite status from the article card’s is_favorited property. If false, call addArticleToFavorites with the article’s ID; on success, update that article’s is_favorited to true. If true, call removeArticleFromFavorites; on success, update is_favorited to false. Optionally update any displayed count of favorites using total_favorites from the response.

Interaction: Open article detail
- Trigger: User clicks an article title or "Read More" control on a card.
- Behavior: Use the article’s ID from the bound Article object and navigate to article.html?id=ARTICLE_ID.

Interaction: URL change via browser navigation
- Trigger: User uses browser Back/Forward buttons, causing URL query parameters to change.
- Behavior: Detect URL changes, re-parse difficulty, rating_min, read_time_max, sort, and page from the URL, update filter controls to match, and call listSafetyBasicsArticles with the new parameters to keep the view consistent with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s authoritative state is derived from the URL query parameters and the responses from getSafetyBasicsFilterOptions and listSafetyBasicsArticles. On every load or URL change, the page parses difficulty, rating_min, read_time_max, sort, and page from the query string. If a parameter is absent, the page uses an internal default, but does not hardcode any option values. For difficulty and rating_min, the UI only allows values that exist in the difficulty_levels and rating_options arrays returned by getSafetyBasicsFilterOptions. The read_time_max filter control is initialized using the read_time_max URL parameter when present; otherwise, it uses the read_time_max_default from getSafetyBasicsFilterOptions. The sort control initializes from the sort URL parameter if it matches one of the sort_options; otherwise, it falls back to an internal default sort that is also reflected in the URL when the user interacts with the page. The page parameter defaults to 1 if not provided or invalid. Whenever a user changes difficulty, rating_min, read_time_max, or sort via the UI, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters and resets page to 1 to avoid out-of-range pagination. Pagination changes only modify the page parameter while leaving other filters and sort untouched. After any URL parameter change, the page constructs a filters object (containing difficulty, rating_min, and read_time_max if present) and a sort value, and calls listSafetyBasicsArticles with these along with the current page and a configured page_size. The is_favorited property for each article comes directly from the listSafetyBasicsArticles response and is updated optimistically when addArticleToFavorites or removeArticleFromFavorites succeed. No state is persisted in localStorage; all persistent state is encoded in the URL parameters or retrieved from the backend via the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page setup and subsequent URL-driven state changes. On load, it parses URL parameters, loads filter options via getSafetyBasicsFilterOptions, initializes filter and sort components, and then calls listSafetyBasicsArticles. It also listens for changes to the URL (e.g., browser Back/Forward) and re-runs the listing query with the new parameters.
data_binding: URL parameters: difficulty, rating_min, read_time_max, sort, page

components.functionality: Provides interactive controls for difficulty, minimum rating, and maximum read time filters. It is populated with options provided by getSafetyBasicsFilterOptions and initialized based on URL parameters. When a user changes any control, it updates the URL parameters, resets the page to 1, and notifies the controller to reload articles.

Displays a sort selection control based on the sort_options returned by getSafetyBasicsFilterOptions. It reflects the current sort URL parameter and allows the user to change the sort order, updating the URL (including resetting page to 1) and triggering a new listSafetyBasicsArticles call.

Summarizes the currently active filters (difficulty, rating_min, read_time_max) in a concise list or chips. It reads current filter values from URL parameters and maps them back to human-readable labels using getSafetyBasicsFilterOptions responses. Users can remove individual filters or clear all filters from this component, which updates URL parameters and triggers a reload of article data.

Renders the list of Safety Basics article cards for the current page of results. It receives the articles array (each containing an Article object and is_favorited flag) from listSafetyBasicsArticles and delegates rendering and interactions to child article-card components.

Represents a single article entry in the list. Displays article metadata (such as title, summary, read time, rating, tags, and difficulty_level) and its favorite status. Provides controls to open the article detail and to toggle favorites. Navigates to article.html?id=ARTICLE_ID on open, and calls the favorites interfaces on favorite toggles.

Shows pagination controls (such as current page, total pages, next/previous) based on total_results, page, and page_size from listSafetyBasicsArticles. When a user selects a different page, it updates the page URL parameter and triggers a refresh of the article list while preserving existing filters and sort.

Centralizes the logic for adding and removing articles from Favorites from within the listing. It receives toggle events from article-card components, calls addArticleToFavorites or removeArticleFromFavorites as appropriate, and then updates the corresponding article’s is_favorited state in the article list.

Displays high-level information above the article list, such as the number of results currently shown and which page is being viewed. It is updated after each listSafetyBasicsArticles call so the user understands how many articles match the current filters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered max-width container (approx. 1100–1200px). Main content column spans roughly 8–9 columns and the sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same two-column editorial layout as the mockup. A centered max-width container holds a dominant left column (articles and controls) and a supporting right sidebar (author/auxiliary info). Within the left column, controls are stacked in a clear top-to-bottom order: results header at the top, then a horizontal band combining filter bar (left) and sort selector (right), followed by a compact applied-filters bar, then a vertical list of article rows, and finally pagination controls. Each article row uses the media-object pattern from the mockup: thumbnail image on the left, text + CTA + favorite action on the right.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same two-column editorial layout as the mockup. A centered max-width container holds a dominant left column (articles and controls) and a supporting right sidebar (author/auxiliary info). Within the left column, controls are stacked in a clear top-to-bottom order: results header at the top, then a horizontal band combining filter bar (left) and sort selector (right), followed by a compact applied-filters bar, then a vertical list of article rows, and finally pagination controls. Each article row uses the media-object pattern from the mockup: thumbnail image on the left, text + CTA + favorite action on the right.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page content is centered in a container about 1100–1200px wide. Inside this container, the main content (filters, articles, pagination) occupies about 70% of the width on the left, and the sidebar occupies the remaining 30% on the right. Within each article row, the thumbnail occupies roughly 30–35% of the row width, with the text area taking the remaining 65–70%. Vertical spacing between major bands (header → results header → controls → article list → pagination) follows the 32–48px rhythm, while internal padding inside interactive bands and cards follows 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious editorial feel. Controls in the filter bar and sort selector are not packed tightly; they have room to breathe within a single horizontal band. The applied-filters bar remains compact but with clearly separated pill-like chips. Article rows are tall enough to give the title, excerpt, CTA, and favorite icon breathing room without feeling sparse. Pagination controls are simple and centered with generous padding around them. Overall, information is easy to scan, with a bias toward legibility over showing maximum filters or metadata at once.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F-pattern. After the global site header, the eye lands on the page-level area at the top of the main column, starting with the results header. From there, attention moves horizontally across the filter bar from left (filters) to right (sort selector), then drops down to applied filters, then into the vertical rhythm of article rows: image on the left, title and excerpt on the right, then down to the marine-blue CTA, and optionally to a heart/favorite icon near the title. After scanning a few rows, the eye naturally shifts to the right column due to its clear separation and the strong top label (e.g., ABOUT THE AUTHOR) in the sidebar. At the bottom of the scroll, the eye comes to rest on the centered pagination controls.

layout.overall_description: The page sits within a centered, white, max-width container that feels like a clean boating safety magazine. A shallow global header spans the top, followed by a comfortable vertical gap before the main two-column content area. On the left, about 70% of the width is dedicated to the Safety Basics listing: a subtle results header at the top, then a horizontal control band where filters stretch across the left and the sort selector anchors the right edge. Just beneath this band, a slim applied-filters strip renders active filters as small pills, then the layout opens into a rhythm of article rows. Each row shows a rectangular thumbnail on the left and a text panel on the right with a bold title, a short excerpt, a bright marine-blue “Continue Reading” button, and a small favorite toggle near the top-right of the text block. Rows are separated by generous vertical spacing, keeping the list airy and scannable. Below the list, centered pagination controls provide simple navigation. To the right, a narrower sidebar column runs from near the top of the main content area downward, starting with an ABOUT THE AUTHOR card—dark label bar on top, large circular avatar centered, and descriptive text below—followed by additional stacked widgets. The overall impression is calm, structured, and easy to read, with subtle alignment and spacing echoing a modern blog rather than a dense application UI.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller is non-visual and sits logically at the top of the main content hierarchy in the DOM, just inside the centered page container but before any visible elements. It does not occupy visual space on the canvas; instead, it initializes data and URL-based state for the Safety Basics page so that when the user lands here, the visible components (results header, filters, list, favorites) all render in their correct positions and states.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; participates in the document flow programmatically only, with no rendered dimensions or on-screen position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar appears in the main left column, directly beneath the results header and above the applied-filters summary. It occupies the full width of the left column (about 70% of the total page width) and sits within a single horizontal band approximately 56–72px tall. Inside this band, filter controls (such as dropdowns, checkboxes, or tags for topics like ‘Life Jackets’, ‘Weather’, ‘Navigation’) are left-aligned, starting roughly 24px from the left edge of the main column. There is 16–24px internal padding on all sides to keep the controls from touching the edges. The right edge of this same band is visually shared with the sort selector, creating a continuous control strip. A very subtle bottom border or light divider line runs under the band to separate it from the applied-filters bar below, mirroring the understated editorial style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows with the document in the main content column. On taller viewports, it may become sticky within the left column—pinning itself just below the global header when the user scrolls—maintaining a consistent horizontal position while allowing the article list to scroll beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort selector shares the same horizontal band as the filter bar, anchored to the far right of the main left column. It occupies roughly 20–30% of that band’s width, depending on viewport size, and aligns vertically centered within the band. The component appears as a compact dropdown or segmented control labeled ‘Sort by’ (e.g., Newest, Most Popular), with its right edge lining up with the right edge of the left column, about 24px in from the page container’s right gutter. A small horizontal gap (16–24px) separates it from the nearest filter controls to the left, keeping the band visually balanced. On narrower screens, the sort control stacks beneath the primary filter controls but retains its right alignment within the column, preserving the F-pattern scanning from left (filters) to right (sort).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Visually and structurally attached to the filter bar; moves with it when the filter band becomes sticky, staying pinned to the top of the main content area as the user scrolls the article list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied-filters summary is a slender bar immediately below the filter/sort band, spanning the full width of the left column. It sits about 8–16px beneath the filter band’s bottom edge, creating a tight but distinct vertical separation. The bar itself has minimal height—around 32–40px—and uses left-aligned filter ‘chips’ or pill-shaped labels to represent active filters. These chips begin at the same left margin as the filter bar (about 24px in from the column edge) and wrap to a second line if needed. There is generous horizontal spacing (8–12px) between chips so each can be quickly scanned. When no filters are applied, this area collapses or shows a faint ‘No filters applied’ label, maintaining the same alignment but consuming less vertical space. The background remains white with perhaps a very subtle grey tint or top border to differentiate it from the article list below without adding visual heaviness.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows normally in the vertical stack. Its height adjusts dynamically to the number of applied filters (wrapping chips to new lines as needed), pushing the article list down while preserving the overall vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article list occupies the main body of the left column, beginning roughly 24–32px below the applied-filters summary. It spans the full width of the left column and consists of a vertical stack of article cards, each following a consistent media-object layout. The list maintains a steady rhythm: each card is separated from the next by about 32–40px of vertical space, allowing the eye to distinguish each article as a separate section. The left edge of all article cards aligns with the left edge of the filter bar and applied-filters bar, and the right edges align with the sort selector and column boundary, reinforcing the columnar structure. There are no additional borders around the entire list; the separation is defined purely by spacing and the internal structure of each article card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block flow within the main column. As filters or sorting change, this region updates while retaining the same spatial footprint, growing vertically when more results are visible and pushing the pagination controls further down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each article card is a horizontal row within the article list, reminiscent of the mockup’s blog post item. The card occupies the full width of the left column and is built as a two-part media object: on the left, a rectangular thumbnail image; on the right, a text and actions panel. The thumbnail sits flush to the card’s left margin, consuming about 30–35% of the card width and maintaining a consistent height across cards (e.g., a 3:2 ratio) to create a tidy vertical rail of images. To the right of the thumbnail, separated by around 24px of horizontal space, the text panel fills the remaining 65–70% width. Inside this panel, a bold, left-aligned article title sits at the top with about 8–16px of space above and below, followed by a two- to three-line excerpt in smaller, comfortable body text. Below the excerpt, aligned to the left, a bright marine-blue ‘CONTINUE READING’ button stands out as the primary CTA, with roughly 16px of vertical spacing above it. A small favorite icon (such as a heart or bookmark) appears in the top-right corner of the text panel, aligned horizontally with the title’s baseline or slightly above it, using the same blue accent when active and a subtle outline when inactive. The entire card rests on the white page background, optionally with a very light bottom divider line; internal padding of 16–24px ensures that text and buttons never touch the card edges or the image. Vertical spacing of about 24–32px below the card separates it from the next card in the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normal block-level behavior within the list. The image and text sections maintain their relative proportions responsively, with the layout collapsing into a vertical stack (image on top, text below) on small screens while preserving consistent padding and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit at the bottom of the left column, directly beneath the last article card with a generous top margin of about 48px to clearly separate navigation from content. This component is horizontally centered within the left column, forming a short row of page numbers and next/previous controls. The control row spans only as wide as its content—roughly one-third of the column width—so there is ample white space to its left and right, reinforcing its role as a footer-like control area rather than another content block. Each clickable element (page number or arrow) has 8–12px of horizontal spacing and 8–16px of vertical padding, with the active page highlighted using the marine-blue accent. Additional margin (32–48px) below the controls separates them from any page-level footer that may follow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored at the end of the article list within the normal scroll flow. It does not stick or float; instead, it becomes visible only when the user scrolls past the content, acting as a natural end-of-list control.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites handler is primarily a behavioral layer but has a clear visual manifestation on each article card. It controls the small favorite icon located at the top-right corner of each article’s text panel. The icon is inset about 16px from the card’s right edge and 16px from the top of the text block, aligning visually with the title while not competing with it. When an article is favorited, the icon fills with the marine-blue accent; when not, it appears as a subtle outlined shape in a neutral grey. On hover or focus, the icon gets a slight color emphasis without enlarging, maintaining the clean flat aesthetic. There is no separate, large favorites panel on this page; instead, the favorites handler quietly coordinates the state across all article cards within the existing layout, ensuring that favorited articles remain visually marked even as filters, sorting, and pagination change.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-block behavioral component that attaches to each article card. Its visible icons remain anchored in the top-right of the text panels and move naturally with the cards during scrolling or reordering.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results header sits at the very top of the left column, immediately beneath the global site header and any page-level title area. It spans the full width of the left column and is left-aligned, beginning about 48px below the bottom edge of the global header to establish a strong top margin and page identity. This header may include a bold text label such as ‘Safety Basics’ or ‘Safety Basics Articles’, possibly accompanied by a lighter-weight line below indicating the number of results (e.g., ‘Showing 12 of 48 safety basics articles’) in smaller type. The primary heading line is prominent but not oversized—roughly matching the mockup’s ‘Posts by Lyle’ style—while the secondary line sits 4–8px below it. There is 24–32px of space between the bottom of the results header and the start of the filter/sort band below, ensuring the header reads as its own page-level introduction before functional controls begin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static page-level element at the top of the main content area. It does not scroll independently or become sticky; it moves off-screen as the user scrolls down, leaving the filter bar and content to occupy the top of the viewport.

name: Article
title: Article
description: Displays a single boating article or guide with full content, metadata, favorites and learning-path actions, and an optional print-friendly view.

core_features: Load and display full article details based on the 'id' URL parameter using getArticleDetail, including content, metadata, and related articles.
Provide a toggle to add or remove the current article from the user's Favorites using addArticleToFavorites and removeArticleFromFavorites.
Allow the user to add the current article as a step in their learning path using addArticleToLearningPath.
Offer a Print-Friendly View mode triggered via a 'Print-Friendly View' control that navigates to article.html with view=print and uses getArticlePrintView.
Show related articles from getArticleDetail as navigable links back to article.html with the appropriate 'id' parameter.
user_workflows: View an article: (1) User navigates to article.html with an 'id' URL parameter. (2) The page controller reads the 'id' and 'view' parameters. (3) If 'view' is absent or not equal to 'print', the controller calls getArticleDetail with the given articleId. (4) When the response returns, the article header, metadata, body content, favorite toggle, learning path control, and related articles list are populated from the response data. (5) The user can scroll through the content and navigate to related articles via their links (which update the 'id' parameter and reload the page).

Add or remove an article from Favorites: (1) On the standard article view, the page shows a favorites control reflecting the is_favorited value from getArticleDetail. (2) If the user clicks the control when the article is not favorited, the page calls addArticleToFavorites with the current articleId. (3) On a successful response, the control updates to the favorited state and may show feedback using the message and total_favorites from the response. (4) If the user clicks the control when the article is favorited, the page calls removeArticleFromFavorites with the current articleId. (5) On success, the control updates to the non-favorited state and may show updated feedback using the returned message and total_favorites.

Add an article to the learning path: (1) On the standard article view, the page shows an 'add to learning path' control. (2) When the user clicks this control, the page calls addArticleToLearningPath with the current articleId. (3) On a successful response, the UI shows confirmation using the message field and can update any local indication that the article is part of the learning path, based on the returned learning_path and learning_path_item objects and total_steps count.

Open Print-Friendly View: (1) On the standard article view, the page shows a 'Print-Friendly View' control near the article title or utilities. (2) When the user activates this control, the browser navigates to article.html with the same 'id' and with 'view=print'. (3) The page controller reads the URL, detects view=print, and calls getArticlePrintView with the given articleId. (4) The returned article_title, print_body_html, and metadata are rendered in a simplified layout that excludes non-essential interactive elements. (5) The user can then use the browser's print function to print the page.

Exit Print-Friendly View and return to full article: (1) In print view mode, the page provides a navigation control (for example, a link) that points to article.html with only the 'id' parameter and without 'view=print'. (2) When the user activates this control, the browser loads article.html in standard mode. (3) The page controller detects that 'view' is not 'print', calls getArticleDetail, and re-renders the full interactive article view.
interactions: Initial page load parses 'id' and 'view' URL parameters and decides whether to call getArticleDetail or getArticlePrintView.
Clicking the favorite toggle control triggers either addArticleToFavorites or removeArticleFromFavorites depending on the current is_favorited state.
Clicking the 'add to learning path' control triggers addArticleToLearningPath for the current articleId.
Clicking the 'Print-Friendly View' control navigates to article.html with the same 'id' and 'view=print', triggering the print-friendly loading logic.
Clicking an 'exit print view' control navigates back to article.html with only the 'id' parameter, returning to the standard view.
Clicking a related article link updates the URL to article.html with a different 'id' parameter, triggering a new getArticleDetail call.
If the 'id' URL parameter is missing or invalid, the page controller sets an error state and the error component displays a generic problem message instead of article content.
page_functionality.state_logic: The core page state is derived entirely from URL parameters and interface responses. The 'id' parameter is mandatory to identify which article to load. On every load or navigation to article.html, the page controller reads 'id' and 'view'. If 'view' is exactly 'print', the page enters print mode and calls getArticlePrintView with the provided articleId; only the print-focused components are rendered. If 'view' is absent or has any other value, the page enters standard mode and calls getArticleDetail. The response from getArticleDetail provides article, is_favorited, category_name, difficulty_label, boat_size_label, and related_articles, which populate the various components and determine control states. The favorite state is not stored in the URL; instead, it is derived from is_favorited on load and then updated in memory according to the results of addArticleToFavorites or removeArticleFromFavorites calls. Learning path membership for the current article is inferred from the response of addArticleToLearningPath when the user performs that action; if needed, the page can maintain an in-memory flag but does not persist it in the URL. Navigation to related articles or between normal and print views always updates the 'id' and 'view' URL parameters and causes a full re-evaluation of state and a fresh interface call. No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used for core state; URL parameters and API data fully drive the behavior.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, determines whether to load standard or print view based on URL parameters, invokes getArticleDetail or getArticlePrintView, manages loading and error states, and passes data to child components.
Displays the article title and key high-level metadata from the standard detail view, such as the category label and optional publish date, and exposes a control to switch to the Print-Friendly View.

Shows detailed metadata for the article in standard view, including read_time_minutes, rating, rating_count if available, difficulty_label, boat_size_label, and tags, using fields from the getArticleDetail response.
Renders the full article body content for standard view using the article.content field from getArticleDetail and ensures it is scrollable and readable.

Displays the current favorite state of the article as a toggleable control and allows the user to add or remove the article from Favorites by calling addArticleToFavorites or removeArticleFromFavorites.
Provides a control for adding the current article to the user's learning path by calling addArticleToLearningPath and then updating local UI state to reflect that the article has been added.

Displays a list of related articles returned from getArticleDetail.related_articles, with each item linking to article.html with its own 'id' parameter so the user can navigate to another article detail page.

Handles the print-friendly article representation when view=print, rendering article_title, print_body_html, and metadata from getArticlePrintView and providing a control to navigate back to the standard article view.
Within print view, displays simplified metadata such as read time, publish date, and tags using the metadata object from getArticlePrintView.
Shows a generic error message when the article cannot be loaded, such as when getArticleDetail or getArticlePrintView fails or when the 'id' parameter is missing or invalid.
Indicates that the article data or print view data is currently being loaded after calling getArticleDetail or getArticlePrintView and hides itself once a response is received.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered max-width container (approx. 1100–1200px). Main content column spans roughly 8–9 columns and the sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses the same two-column editorial layout as the mockup. Below the global site header, the Article page controller centers a max-width content container on the page. Inside this container, the left column (~70% width) holds the article header, metadata strip, favorites and learning-path controls (placed near the top as lightweight tools), and the full article body. The right column (~30% width) acts as a sidebar and stacks a compact tools card (favorites and learning-path in sidebar form, if desired) and a Related Articles list that mimics the media-object list pattern from the mockup. Supporting states (loading and error) are presented in the main column area, replacing the article content as needed. A separate print-view layout reflows all content into a single, centered column with no sidebar, optimized for linear reading and printing.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses the same two-column editorial layout as the mockup. Below the global site header, the Article page controller centers a max-width content container on the page. Inside this container, the left column (~70% width) holds the article header, metadata strip, favorites and learning-path controls (placed near the top as lightweight tools), and the full article body. The right column (~30% width) acts as a sidebar and stacks a compact tools card (favorites and learning-path in sidebar form, if desired) and a Related Articles list that mimics the media-object list pattern from the mockup. Supporting states (loading and error) are presented in the main column area, replacing the article content as needed. A separate print-view layout reflows all content into a single, centered column with no sidebar, optimized for linear reading and printing.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header remains a shallow band at the top (around 10–15% of the initial viewport height). Beneath it, the article-page-controller centers a container approximately 1100–1200px wide with generous left/right margins on larger screens. Inside the container, the main article column occupies about 70% of the container width and the sidebar 30%, separated by at least 32px of horizontal space. Vertical spacing between major blocks (header, metadata, controls, body, related content) follows the 32–48px rhythm; sub-elements within blocks (buttons, labels, paragraphs) use 8–24px spacing. The print view collapses everything into a single column of similar width but uses slightly tighter vertical spacing to reduce wasted space on paper while retaining readability.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density follows the mockup’s moderately spacious editorial feel: plenty of whitespace around the article title and between sections, comfortable paragraph spacing in the body, and clear separation between the main column and sidebar. Within the sidebar, widgets (favorites/learning-path tools and related articles) are distinct cards with labeled headers and 16–24px internal padding. The related articles list is simple: small thumbnail, title, perhaps a single-line description, and no crowded metadata, mirroring the mockup’s restrained presentation. Loading and error states are minimal and centered, without extra clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves in an F-pattern. After the site header, the user’s attention lands on the large article title in the main column, then drops to the metadata strip immediately below. To the right of or just below the metadata, the favorites toggle and learning-path controls stand out with the marine-blue accent, encouraging interaction. From there, the reader flows down through the article body. As the reader scrolls or completes the article, the eye is drawn rightward toward the sidebar by a subtle vertical division and the darker label bar at the top of the Related Articles widget, then scans down through those teasers. In print view, the flow becomes purely top-to-bottom: title, print metadata block, article body, then any related articles rendered as a simple linear section at the end.

layout.overall_description: The Article page presents a clean, two-column editorial layout centered within a wide but contained canvas on a white background. At the top sits the site-wide header band with logo, menu label on the left, and search icon on the right, occupying only a small slice of vertical space so the article dominates the screen. Below this, the main content container is clearly defined: on the left, a broad main column hosts a bold, left-aligned article title, followed by a slim metadata strip, then compact favorites and learning-path controls, and finally the full article body with generous line spacing and comfortable margins. On the right, a narrower sidebar column begins a little below the article title, holding vertically stacked cards—first any tool-oriented widget (for user actions) and then a Related Articles card that uses a dark marine-blue label bar and a vertical list of articles with thumbnail-and-title media objects. There is consistent vertical rhythm between sections, with 32–48px of whitespace separating major modules so each block feels distinct yet related. When the print view is activated, the layout simplifies: the sidebar disappears, and the article reflows into a single centered column with the title at the top, a compact print metadata block under it, and the body flowing linearly below, providing a quiet, legible, and ink-efficient reading experience. Loading and error states temporarily replace the main column content area, sitting centered in the same visual frame so the layout remains stable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-page-controller spans the full width of the viewport directly beneath the global site header and is responsible for establishing the centered, max-width content area. It creates a main container approximately 1100–1200px wide, centered horizontally with generous left and right margins on large screens. Within this container, it defines a two-column grid: the left main column occupies about 70% of the container width and the right sidebar about 30%, separated by roughly 32px of gutter space. All article components (article-header, article-metadata-strip, favorites-toggle-control, learning-path-add-control, article-body-content) are arranged in a vertical stack within the left column, while the related-articles-list sits in the right column as a stacked card or widget. The controller also manages state-based layouts: when loading-indicator or error-message-block are active, they are centered within the main column area and suppress the normal article content; when the print-view-page is activated, it temporarily replaces the two-column layout with a single, full-width column that hides or ignores the sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines responsive behavior: two-column layout on medium and large screens, collapsing to a single column on small screens where the sidebar content (including related-articles-list) flows beneath the main article content. Static in scroll (not fixed), but controls visibility and stacking of loading, error, and print views.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-header sits at the top of the left main column, immediately below any breadcrumb or page-level label (if present) and clearly above all other article components. It is left-aligned and spans the full width of the main column, with at least 24–32px of padding from the top of the content container to create a sense of introduction. The title text is large and bold, occupying roughly 2–3 lines at most and leaving plenty of white space above and below. There is about 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom of the article-header and the article-metadata-strip beneath it. The header does not visually intrude into the sidebar; instead, it sets the primary focal point in the left column that the sidebar widgets visually align to vertically just below its baseline.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, topmost element in the main article column; it scrolls with the page without any sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-metadata-strip appears directly beneath the article-header within the left column, spanning the full width of that column. It is a low-profile horizontal band containing compact metadata such as author name, publication date, reading time, or category. The strip uses a subtle visual treatment—a faint bottom border or a very light gray background—with 8–12px of vertical padding inside to keep it airy but unobtrusive. Text is left-aligned, with items spaced horizontally using 16px gaps. There is about 16px of vertical space between the bottom of this strip and the controls (favorites-toggle-control and learning-path-add-control) that follow. The strip’s height is visually slim, allowing the attention to move quickly from the bold title above into the actionable tools and then down into the article body.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal band within the main column; it maintains its position in the vertical flow with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-body-content occupies the majority of the left column below the metadata strip and the immediate controls. It starts approximately 24–32px below the controls to create a clear separation between meta/actions and reading content. This block spans the full width of the main column but maintains 16–24px internal spacing from any media elements to the text, ensuring an open, readable feel. Paragraphs are arranged in a single column with a comfortable line length (approximately 60–75 characters) and 8–16px spacing between paragraphs. Inline images or diagrams are centered or left-aligned within the column and never encroach into the sidebar. Headings within the body create secondary visual anchors and maintain at least 24px of spacing above and 16px below. The overall height of the body content is determined by article length, extending downward and pushing the sidebar content to match or exceed the article’s scroll depth. At the end of the body, there is 32–48px of vertical space before any full-width post-article elements (if added) or before the footer region, preserving the editorial rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, long-scrolling content area within the left column; no fixed elements inside. On small screens, it becomes the first full-width block, with sidebar-type components stacking beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-toggle-control is positioned in the upper portion of the main article column, just below the article-metadata-strip and slightly above the start of the article body. It sits either inline with the learning-path-add-control or directly above it, forming a compact, horizontally aligned pair anchored to the right side of the main column. The control itself is a small, unobtrusive button or icon-with-label (e.g., a star icon with 'Save to favorites') that uses the marine-blue accent color to signal interactivity. There is approximately 8–12px of space between this control and the learning-path-add-control when they are stacked horizontally, or 16px if stacked vertically. Vertically, the controls block is separated from the metadata strip by about 16px and from the article body by 24px, giving it enough room to be noticed without overwhelming the text flow. On narrow screens, this control shifts to full-width alignment above the article body, left-aligned but still visually compact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column; it scrolls away with the content. On smaller screens, it reflows from right-aligned inline placement to its own row above the body content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The learning-path-add-control is placed adjacent to the favorites-toggle-control in the same small action block between the metadata strip and the article body. It is aligned either to the immediate left of the favorites control (if space allows) or directly beneath it in a tight vertical stack, with 8–12px of horizontal or vertical spacing between the two. Like the favorites control, it uses the marine-blue accent but in a slightly different treatment (e.g., outlined button if favorites is solid, or vice versa) to distinguish their purposes while keeping a cohesive look. The entire action set is anchored towards the right edge of the main column, respecting a 24px inset from the column’s right boundary, so it doesn’t feel cramped. There is a clear 24px gap below this control before the first paragraph of the article body to separate tools from content. On small screens, it aligns under the favorites control full-width, maintaining at least 8–12px gap between the two controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, reflowing with the favorites-toggle-control as part of the same responsive action area: right-aligned inline on wider screens, stacked in a vertical block above the article body on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-articles-list resides within the right sidebar column, starting near the top of the column but slightly lower than the baseline of the article-header so it doesn’t compete directly with the main title. It is presented as a distinct card or widget that spans the full width of the sidebar. At the very top of this card is a dark marine-blue label bar with white or light-text reading 'Related Articles', mirroring the 'ABOUT THE AUTHOR' bar from the mockup. This bar is about 32–40px tall with 8–12px of vertical padding and stretches edge-to-edge within the card. Beneath the label bar, the related articles are listed vertically in 2–5 rows. Each related article row follows a compact media-object pattern: on the left, a small rectangular thumbnail occupies about 30–35% of the row width; on the right, the remaining 65–70% displays the article title (and optionally a short one-line description) stacked top-to-bottom. Each row is separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing, and the entire card has 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. There is at least 32px of vertical space between this widget and any other sidebar content above or below. On small screens, the related-articles-list moves below the article body and expands to full content width, keeping the same internal structure but feeling like a natural continuation section at the bottom of the article.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar widget in a vertical stack; it scrolls alongside the main column content. On smaller screens, it reflows to a full-width block following the article body.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The print-view-page is an alternate layout that takes over the content region when a print-friendly mode is activated. It discards the two-column structure and renders a single, centered column occupying roughly 70% of the viewport width (similar to the original main column but allowed to shift depending on print margins). The page begins with the article title at the top, centered horizontally or left-aligned depending on the final print style, with generous 24–32px spacing above and below. Directly beneath the title sits the print-view-metadata-block, followed by the full article body content. Sidebar elements such as the related-articles-list can optionally be rendered as a simplified section at the bottom, using full-width linear listing. Interactive controls (favorites-toggle-control, learning-path-add-control, search, menu) are omitted or muted, ensuring the print layout is purely textual with occasional images. The background remains white, and extraneous borders or shadows are minimized to reduce visual noise on paper.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the normal article layout. When active, it replaces the main container and hides the sidebar, resulting in a single flowing column optimized for printing; it scrolls normally in the browser but is intended for print/export.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the print-view-page, the print-view-metadata-block appears immediately below the article title, spanning the full width of the print column. It is a compact rectangular section containing key metadata required for printed reference (e.g., author, publication date, URL, version, or source attribution). This block has a subtle border at the top and bottom or a very light gray background, with approximately 12–16px of padding on all sides to clearly separate it from both the title above and the body text below. Text is left-aligned and arranged in short lines or a two-column text layout only if space permits, but overall it remains compact and easy to skim. There is around 16–24px of space between the bottom of this block and the first paragraph of the article body in the print view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static part of the linear print layout, fixed in position directly under the article title and above the body within print-view-page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-message-block appears in the main content area when the article fails to load or some other critical issue occurs. It is centered horizontally within the same main container defined by the article-page-controller and typically replaces both columns’ content visually. The block itself is a medium-width card (around 60–70% of the container width), aligned centrally with generous 24–32px padding inside. It includes a concise error title, brief explanatory text, and possibly a small call-to-action (e.g., 'Retry') aligned left. There is ample whitespace above and below—about 48px separating it from the site header and 32–48px from any footer elements—so it stands out as the primary focus when active. The sidebar content and article body are hidden or visually suppressed while this block is displayed, keeping the user’s attention on the message.
