Within each learning-path-step-row, the remove control is placed in the right content block, typically aligned to the top-right or near the primary 'Open lesson' button but styled with lower visual weight (e.g., small trash icon or muted text link). It sits in the same horizontal band as the CTA or just below it, ensuring that the reading flow moves from title to CTA first, with remove controls only appearing as secondary options. There is enough padding (8–12px) around the control to prevent accidental taps or clicks.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with other controls; it does not create its own structural block. It scrolls with the toolbar or row it belongs to and does not change layout dimensions when shown or hidden aside from its own minimal footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation block is placed at the very bottom of the left main column, directly after the learning-path-list or the learning-path-empty-state. It begins with about 32–48px of vertical space above it to clearly signal the end of the main list. The block itself spans the full width of the main column but remains visually light: it is a simple vertical stack of 2–3 text links, left-aligned, such as 'Browse more lessons' or 'Back to dashboard'. Each link has 8–12px of vertical spacing between them, with no heavy container or card; at most a subtle top border may separate this block from the list above. This understated block anchors the bottom of the page and supports the F-pattern by offering logical next steps once the user finishes scanning or editing their path.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky set of links that appear in normal document flow; they scroll into and out of view with the rest of the main column content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error banner appears near the top of the content area, directly below the global header and above the learning-path-summary and toolbar. It spans the full width of the centered content container, crossing both the left main column and the right sidebar so that error messages feel global to the page. Visually, it is a low, horizontal strip with a soft red-tinted background, a thin darker-red left border, and 12–16px of vertical padding. The error icon and headline are left-aligned within the banner, with supporting text to the right or wrapping below on smaller screens. There is about 16–24px of vertical margin above and below the banner to keep it distinct from the header and the summary panel. Despite its alerting color, its overall height remains shallow to avoid overpowering the primary content while still being immediately noticeable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static panel that sits in the normal flow at the top of the content container; when present, it pushes all other content down. It scrolls away with the rest of the page and is not fixed to the viewport.

name: Checklists
title: Checklists
description: Page for browsing boating safety and trip-preparation checklists with filter and pagination controls, and opening a selected checklist for detailed completion.

core_features: Retrieve and display available filter options for boat type, trip length, and passenger range using getChecklistsFilterOptions.
List checklists with titles and descriptions using listChecklists, applying filters from the URL (boat_type, trip_length, passenger_range) and pagination (page).
Allow users to change filters via form controls and update the URL parameters, then reload the checklist listing accordingly.
Provide pagination controls based on total_results, page, and page_size from listChecklists, updating the page URL parameter.
Highlight recommended checklists (using Checklist.is_recommended) within the current results set.
Allow navigation to the checklist detail page (checklist_detail.html) when a user selects a checklist.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Arrive on Checklists page from any source
1. Browser loads checklists.html with optional URL parameters boat_type, trip_length, passenger_range, and page.
2. Page calls getChecklistsFilterOptions to obtain filter option lists for boat types, trip lengths, and passenger ranges.
3. Page reads URL parameters and validates them against the returned filter options. Invalid or missing values are treated as 'no filter' for that dimension.
4. Page constructs a filters object from the valid URL parameters and determines the current page number (defaulting to the first page if page is missing or invalid).
5. Page calls listChecklists with the filters object, current page, and a configured page_size.
6. When listChecklists returns, the page renders the checklist listing, recommended highlights (based on is_recommended within the returned items), and pagination controls.
7. The user can click any checklist title or explicit action to navigate to checklist_detail.html with the appropriate id parameter.

Workflow 2: Adjust filters to narrow down checklists
1. User interacts with the filter controls (boat type, trip length, passenger range) and selects or clears values.
2. On each change, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters (boat_type, trip_length, passenger_range) to match the current selections and sets page to the first page.
3. The browser updates the URL query string; the page logic detects this state change (via history update or explicit handler) and constructs a new filters object from the updated URL.
4. Page calls listChecklists with the new filters and page 1.
5. The checklist listing, recommended highlights, and pagination are re-rendered according to the new results.
6. The user can share or bookmark the URL to preserve the current filter configuration.

Workflow 3: Use pagination to browse more checklists
1. User views the current checklist results and scrolls to pagination controls.
2. User clicks a specific page number, 'Next', or 'Previous' control.
3. The page updates the page URL parameter to the chosen page number while preserving any current boat_type, trip_length, and passenger_range parameters.
4. Page reconstructs the filters object from the URL and calls listChecklists with the updated page value.
5. New results are rendered; recommended checklists within this page are highlighted.
6. Browser back/forward controls navigate through previous page states by using the URL parameters.

Workflow 4: Open a checklist for detailed completion
1. User identifies a checklist of interest in the listing.
2. User clicks either the checklist title or an explicit 'Open Checklist' action in that list row.
3. The page issues a navigation to checklist_detail.html, passing the selected Checklist.id as the id URL parameter.
4. The checklist_detail.html page (defined elsewhere) loads and can use the id parameter to display and manage the detailed checklist and progress.
interactions: Filter selection: User selects or clears a boat type from a filter control. The page updates the boat_type URL parameter to the selected filter value (or removes it if cleared), resets page to 1, and re-calls listChecklists with the new filters.
Trip length selection: User chooses a trip length. The page updates the trip_length URL parameter (or removes it), resets page to 1, and re-calls listChecklists.
Passenger range selection: User chooses a passenger range. The page updates the passenger_range URL parameter (or removes it), resets page to 1, and re-calls listChecklists.
Filter initialization: On page load, the filter controls initialize their selected values based on the current URL parameters after validating them against getChecklistsFilterOptions results.
Pagination click: User clicks a page number, 'Next', or 'Previous'. The page updates the page URL parameter and re-calls listChecklists with the same filters but a different page value.
Checklist item click: User clicks a checklist title or 'Open Checklist' button on a list item. The page navigates to checklist_detail.html?id=<Checklist.id>.

Recommended checklist emphasis: When results include checklists where is_recommended is true, the page emphasizes those items (for example, by grouping or flagging them) within the listing, without altering the underlying data.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Checklists page uses URL query parameters as the source of truth for filter and pagination state. On every load or state change, the page reads boat_type, trip_length, passenger_range, and page from the URL and uses these to build the filters passed into listChecklists.

Initialization:
- On initial load of checklists.html, the page reads the current query string.
- It calls getChecklistsFilterOptions with no parameters to obtain valid option lists for boat_types, trip_lengths, and passenger_ranges.
- It validates each of the filter-related URL parameters against the corresponding option lists. If a value does not match any option, that parameter is ignored for filtering and the corresponding control is shown as unselected.
- The effective filter state is constructed from the valid URL parameter values only.
- The page parameter is parsed as a positive integer; if missing or invalid, the first page is assumed for the listChecklists call.

URL as single source of truth:
- Filter controls do not maintain independent persistent state; instead, they reflect the state encoded in the URL parameters.
- When a user changes a filter control, the page updates the browser's URL query string to reflect the new filter state (adding, changing, or removing the boat_type, trip_length, and passenger_range parameters as appropriate) and sets page=1. No state is stored in localStorage or other client-side storage.
- When the URL is updated (via history APIs or full navigation), the page re-derives filters from the URL and calls listChecklists with filters and page derived solely from the URL.

Pagination:
- The current page is always represented by the page URL parameter. Clicking pagination controls updates this parameter while keeping any existing filter parameters unchanged.
- listChecklists is called with the current filters and page from the URL, and the returned total_results and page_size values are used to compute the number of pages and the enabled/disabled state of pagination controls.

Highlighting recommended checklists:
- Recommended status is derived directly from the Checklist.is_recommended field in the listChecklists response for the current page. No additional URL parameters are required for this behavior.
- The page does not depend on any client-side stored flags or static lists to determine recommendations; it only uses the data returned from listChecklists on each request.

components.functionality: Initializes and manages filter controls for boat type, trip length, and passenger range, synchronizing them with URL parameters and triggering checklist listing updates.
Displays the current page of checklist results returned by listChecklists, including title, description, and relevant attributes; provides per-item actions to open checklist details.
Derives a subset of recommended checklists from the current listChecklists results (where is_recommended is true) and presents them with additional emphasis within the listing context.
Renders pagination controls based on total_results, page, and page_size from listChecklists, updates the page URL parameter when users navigate between pages, and re-requests the checklist list.
Detects when listChecklists returns zero checklists for the current filters and displays a functional message or prompt, while still allowing users to adjust filters.

Manages synchronization between the URL query parameters and in-page state by reading parameters on load, updating them when filters or pagination change, and triggering listChecklists and getChecklistsFilterOptions calls as needed.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered max-width container (approx. 1100–1200px). Main content column spans roughly 8–9 columns and the sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s classic two‑column blog layout. Below the site header and page title ('Checklists'), the main content area is split into a dominant left column for the interactive checklist list and a narrower right sidebar for secondary content. In the left column, content flows top-to-bottom: filters bar at the very top of the content area, then the checklist listing rendered as repeated media-object rows (thumbnail on the left, text + CTA on the right), followed by pagination controls at the bottom. If no results are found, the empty state replaces the listing and pagination in that same main column. The right sidebar stacks vertical widgets, with the recommended-checklists strip presented as a card-like widget that visually echoes the 'About the author' pattern from the mockup (label bar on top, list underneath). The URL state manager is non-visual and does not occupy physical space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s classic two‑column blog layout. Below the site header and page title ('Checklists'), the main content area is split into a dominant left column for the interactive checklist list and a narrower right sidebar for secondary content. In the left column, content flows top-to-bottom: filters bar at the very top of the content area, then the checklist listing rendered as repeated media-object rows (thumbnail on the left, text + CTA on the right), followed by pagination controls at the bottom. If no results are found, the empty state replaces the listing and pagination in that same main column. The right sidebar stacks vertical widgets, with the recommended-checklists strip presented as a card-like widget that visually echoes the 'About the author' pattern from the mockup (label bar on top, list underneath). The URL state manager is non-visual and does not occupy physical space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The entire page content sits inside a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px) with generous horizontal gutters (24–32px) on large screens. The main content area below the header is split into a ~70% width left column and a ~30% width right sidebar using an implicit 12-column grid (left: 8–9 columns, right: 3–4 columns). Within the left column, the filters bar spans 100% of that column surface, followed by checklist rows that each allocate roughly 30–35% of their width to the thumbnail on the left and 65–70% to the text and CTA on the right. Vertical spacing between major sections (header → filters → listing → pagination, and between stacked sidebar widgets) uses 32–48px, with 16–24px padding inside cards. The header remains a relatively shallow band at the top (~10–15% of the initial viewport height).
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout balances clarity with breathing room, preserving a moderately spacious, editorial density. Each checklist entry is a single row with one thumbnail image, one title, a concise excerpt, and a single prominent 'View checklist' style CTA—no dense metadata clusters. Row heights are consistent and generous, driven by the thumbnail height and a 2–3 line excerpt. The filters bar is simple and horizontally laid out, avoiding overly compact filter chips; controls are comfortably spaced using 8–16px gaps. Sidebar content is minimal and easy to scan, with the recommended strip showing only a small set of highlighted checklists with ample padding and whitespace between items. This keeps the focus on legibility and quick scanning rather than packing in maximum data.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows the F-pattern from the mockup. First, the user sees the global header (logo centered, navigation/search at the sides), then the page heading 'Checklists' centered or slightly left-weighted. From there, the eye drops to the left to the checklist-filters-bar, which runs horizontally and defines the main interactive entry point. The user then scans downward through the checklist-listing rows, moving left-to-right within each row (thumbnail → bold title → supporting copy → bright marine-blue CTA). After a few rows, the gaze naturally shifts right, drawn by the vertical division and the bold label bar of the recommended-checklists-strip in the sidebar, then scrolls downward through any remaining sidebar widgets. Pagination controls at the bottom center of the left column provide a clear end-stop and next-step. When present, the empty state occupies the same primary area as the listing and becomes the main focal point.

layout.overall_description: The Checklists page reads like a clean, minimalist boating journal laid out in a modern two-column blog format. A light global header spans the top, with a centered boat-themed logo, simple navigation, and a search affordance that echo the original mockup. Below, within a centered max-width container, the page title 'Checklists' appears with generous top and bottom spacing, establishing the page context. Under this heading, the layout splits into a dominant left content column and a slimmer right sidebar. At the top of the left column, a horizontal filters bar stretches across the available width, giving users immediate control over topics like safety, trip type, or boat size. Beneath it, a vertical stack of checklist rows fills most of the page: each row is a balanced media object with a rectangular thumbnail on the left and a right-hand block containing a bold checklist name, a short descriptive excerpt, and a bright marine-blue CTA button that invites users to view or start a checklist. Rows are evenly spaced with consistent rhythm, forming a calm, scannable column. At the bottom of this column, centered pagination controls provide navigation through additional pages of checklists. The right sidebar starts near the top of the listing area and runs down the page as a vertical stack of cards; the recommended-checklists strip appears as a framed widget with a dark label bar at the top and a small list of highlighted checklists below, echoing the mockup’s editorial sidebar pattern. White background, straightforward sans-serif type, and restrained marine-blue accents keep the layout airy and practical, while the F-pattern flow naturally guides the user from header to filters to main list, and then into the supporting sidebar content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checklist-filters-bar sits at the top of the main content area in the left column, directly below the page title 'Checklists' and aligned with the left edge of the content column (approximately the 2nd grid column from the left of the page) and ending at the right edge of that column (around the 10th grid column). It spans the full width of the left column (~70% of the page’s content width) and has a low, toolbar-like height defined by its controls rather than by a heavy visual frame. Vertically, it is separated from the page title by about 32–48px of whitespace, and from the first checklist row by about 24–32px. Filters are laid out horizontally from left to right: on the far left, an optional label like 'Filter checklists' or key dropdown (e.g., 'Trip type') anchors the bar; to its right, additional dropdowns, pill-shaped filter chips, or toggle controls are evenly spaced with 8–16px gaps. Controls are aligned so their baselines form a clean horizontal line, and they are vertically centered within a subtle, full-width container with 16px top/bottom padding and 24px left/right padding. The bar uses a very light outline or bottom border to separate it from the white background, with the marine-blue accent reserved for active filter states and the primary 'Clear filters' link, ensuring it feels functional but not visually heavy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The filters bar is positioned within the regular document flow but becomes sticky at the top of the viewport once the user scrolls past its original position, remaining attached to the top edge of the content area (beneath the global header) while the checklist list scrolls underneath. Its width and horizontal position remain locked to the left column, so on wider screens it never extends into the sidebar area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checklist-listing occupies the main vertical space in the left column, immediately below the filters bar. It starts about 24–32px beneath the bottom edge of the filters bar and runs downward almost to the fold and beyond, depending on content length. The listing is a single column of repeated media-object rows. Each row is a horizontal rectangle spanning the full width of the left column (minus internal padding), with 24px of padding on the left and right and 16–24px on the top and bottom. Inside each row, the content is split into two vertical sections: on the left, a fixed-aspect ratio thumbnail image (e.g., 16:9 or 4:3) occupies roughly 30–35% of the row width, aligned flush with the row’s left padding; on the right, a text block takes up the remaining 65–70% of the width, separated by a consistent 24px gutter. The text block is structured top to bottom: a bold checklist title at the top, a short 1–3 line excerpt beneath it with 8–12px spacing, and a single marine-blue 'VIEW CHECKLIST' or 'START CHECKLIST' button aligned to the left under the excerpt, separated by 16px of vertical space. Each row is separated from the next by 32–40px of vertical whitespace, maintaining a steady rhythm. The rows do not use heavy drop shadows; instead, a subtle bottom divider or very light card border may be used to define their edges. On narrower viewports where the layout becomes single-column, the image stacks above the text, preserving a clear vertical flow while keeping proportional padding. The entire listing scrolls vertically with the page, serving as the core content users scan through.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The listing participates in normal document flow, scrolling with the page underneath the sticky filters bar and static header. On responsive breakpoints where the sidebar collapses below the main content, the listing expands to near full container width (minus global side paddings). No fixed or floating behavior is applied to individual rows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recommended-checklists-strip lives in the right-hand sidebar as a card-like widget, visually similar in structure to the mockup’s 'ABOUT THE AUTHOR' card. The sidebar begins near the top of the main content area, so the top edge of this strip aligns roughly with the top of the first few checklist rows in the left column, separated from them by a clear vertical dividing gutter of about 32–40px. The strip’s container spans the full width of the sidebar (~30% of the page content width) and uses 16–24px internal padding. At its very top, a horizontal label bar runs full width, about 40–48px high, with a dark or marine-blue background and white all-caps text like 'RECOMMENDED CHECKLISTS', visually anchoring the widget and echoing the mockup’s strong sidebar header. Below this label bar, the content area lists a small number (e.g., 3–5) of recommended checklists arranged vertically. Each recommended item is a compact row with the title in a bold or semi-bold weight, optionally a single supporting line (like 'Perfect for weekend trips'), and a subtle inline arrow or tiny marine-blue pill link aligned to the right, all separated by 12–16px vertical spacing. The items are left-aligned, preserving a neat vertical reading line, and the overall card is separated from any sidebar content above or below it with 32–40px of vertical margin. The card has a light border or faint background tint to distinguish it from the pure white page background while maintaining the minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The recommended strip scrolls naturally with the sidebar and does not stick or float. On smaller screen sizes where the layout collapses to a single column, this widget moves below the main checklist listing and spans the full container width, keeping its label bar and internal structure but adapting its width to match the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checklist-pagination controls sit at the bottom of the left column, directly beneath the last checklist row in the listing. There is a clear 32–40px vertical gap between the bottom edge of the final row and the top of the pagination area, creating a visual pause and signaling that the list has ended. The pagination area spans the full width of the left column, but its interactive elements are centered horizontally, consistent with the editorial feel. The controls may appear as a row of page numbers with previous/next arrows, or a simpler 'Previous' / 'Next' pair, spaced with 8–16px gaps and using the marine-blue accent for the active page or primary button. The hit targets are generously sized, with at least 32px height. The pagination row itself remains relatively shallow, with only modest top and bottom padding (12–16px), to keep it feeling like a lightweight control rather than a heavy section. Below the pagination, additional vertical whitespace (24–32px) separates it from any subsequent content or footer, maintaining the page’s open rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The pagination is part of the normal vertical flow and scrolls with the content. It does not stick to the bottom of the viewport. When the empty state is active, the pagination is hidden or disabled so that the empty state occupies the end of the content column without competing controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checklist-empty-state occupies the same visual real estate as the checklist listing in the left column when no results match the selected filters. It is positioned directly below the filters bar, with a similar 24–32px gap as the listing would typically have. The empty state is centered within the left column both horizontally and visually: the main message block sits roughly in the upper half of the visible area when first displayed, giving the feeling of an intentional, designed panel rather than a small notice at the top. The content is arranged in a simple vertical stack: at the top, an icon or small illustration (potentially a subtle boat or checklist graphic) centered and sized modestly (~25–30% of the column width); below it, a concise headline like 'No checklists match your filters' in a slightly larger, bold type; then a short explanatory line in regular weight, followed by one or two clear actions. Primary actions, such as 'Reset filters' or 'View all checklists', are presented as a marine-blue button aligned to the center, with 16–24px vertical spacing between each element. The panel uses ample whitespace around the content (at least 48px top and bottom padding within the implied card area) so it feels calm and intentional. No listing rows or pagination controls appear when this state is active, keeping the layout uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The empty state is conditionally rendered in place of the listing and pagination in the normal document flow. It scrolls with the page like any other content. There is no sticky or floating behavior; on smaller screens, it remains centered within the single-column layout, with its width adapting to the container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-manager is non-visual and therefore has no direct spatial footprint on the page. Conceptually, it is tied to the filters bar and pagination in the left column, synchronizing the current filter selections, current page number, and any selected checklist state with the browser’s URL parameters. This ensures that when users share or revisit the URL, the state of the checklist list (filters, page, and possibly sort order) is restored. While invisible, it affects how the visible components behave together: changing filters in the top bar or clicking pagination controls updates both the listing below and the URL, and loading a URL with preset parameters configures the filters and listing accordingly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The URL state manager has no visual or spatial behavior. It operates at the application logic level, listening to events from the filters bar and pagination and responding to URL changes, without altering the layout or occupying space in the rendered interface.

name: Checklist Detail
title: Checklist Detail
description: Displays a single boating checklist with sections and items, lets users mark items complete, and save their progress for later using the provided interfaces.

core_features: Load a specific checklist using the 'id' URL parameter and the getChecklistDetailWithProgress interface.
Display the checklist title and description along with all of its sections and items in checklist order.
Allow users to mark individual checklist items as completed or not completed via checkboxes.
Show a running completion summary (e.g., completed items vs. total items) based on current checkbox state.
Display saved status (saved/unsaved) and the last saved timestamp for the checklist using data from getChecklistDetailWithProgress and saveChecklistProgress.
Provide a prominent 'Save Checklist' action that calls saveChecklistProgress with the current set of completed item IDs.
Indicate when there are unsaved changes since the last load or save.
Allow navigation back to the list of all checklists (checklists.html) via a breadcrumb or back link.
user_workflows:
View a checklist from the checklist list:
1. User clicks a checklist on checklists.html, which navigates to checklist_detail.html?id={checklistId}.
2. On page load, the data loader reads the 'id' URL parameter and calls getChecklistDetailWithProgress(checklistId).
3. The page displays the checklist header (title, description, saved status), sections, and items with their completion state derived from the response.
4. User scrolls the page and reviews sections and items.

Mark items complete and track progress:
1. User clicks a checkbox next to a checklist item in any section.
2. The checklist section list updates the in-memory completion state for that item.
3. The completion summary in the header and/or action bar recalculates based on the updated set of completed items.
4. The page marks the checklist as having unsaved changes (e.g., changing a 'Saved' indicator to 'Unsaved changes' state) until the user saves.

Save checklist progress for later:
1. After marking items complete/incomplete, the user clicks the 'Save Checklist' button in the save bar.
2. The save bar collects the IDs of all items currently marked complete and calls saveChecklistProgress(checklistId, completedItemIds).
3. If the save is successful, the save bar and header update to show the new last_saved_at timestamp from the returned ChecklistProgress, and the state is marked as saved (no unsaved changes).
4. If the save fails, the user sees an error message from the response message field, and the unsaved state remains.

Return to a checklist with saved progress:
1. At a later time, the user navigates back to checklist_detail.html?id={checklistId} either from checklists.html or a direct bookmarked link.
2. The data loader calls getChecklistDetailWithProgress(checklistId).
3. The response includes is_completed flags for each item and last_saved_at; all items are pre-checked according to the saved completion state.
4. The saved status indicator shows that the checklist has previously saved progress along with the last saved time.
5. The user can adjust checkboxes and save again, creating updated progress.

Switch to another checklist via URL change:
1. The user navigates to a different checklist by following a link that updates the URL to checklist_detail.html?id={anotherChecklistId}.
2. The data loader detects the change to the 'id' URL parameter and calls getChecklistDetailWithProgress(anotherChecklistId).
3. All components (header, sections, save bar) re-bind to the new checklist data and reset their internal unsaved-change state based on the new response.
interactions: On page load or when the 'id' URL parameter changes, a data-loader component calls getChecklistDetailWithProgress using the provided checklistId and updates shared page state with the returned checklist, sections, items, and saved status.

When a user clicks an item checkbox, the item’s completion state is toggled in page state; dependent components (completion summary, save button enabled/disabled state, unsaved-change indicator) receive updated state and re-render.

When the user clicks the 'Save Checklist' button, the page gathers all item IDs currently marked complete and calls saveChecklistProgress with checklistId and completedItemIds; then it updates saved status and last_saved_at based on the returned checklist_progress.
If saveChecklistProgress returns an error (success = false), the page displays the message from the response and leaves the unsaved changes indicator active.
Clicking the 'Back to all checklists' breadcrumb or link navigates the browser to checklists.html without additional parameters.

If getChecklistDetailWithProgress fails (for example, due to an invalid checklistId), the page shows a functional error state (e.g., an error message and disabled interactive elements) while still allowing navigation back to checklists.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by the 'id' URL parameter (checklist_detail.html?id={checklistId}). When 'id' is present and valid, the page calls getChecklistDetailWithProgress(id) to obtain the Checklist, its sections and items, and the saved completion status (is_saved and last_saved_at). If the 'id' parameter changes while the user is on the page (e.g., via in-app navigation), the data loader re-invokes getChecklistDetailWithProgress with the new value and resets all internal completion and unsaved-change state based on the new response. Unsaved changes (checkbox toggles not yet persisted via saveChecklistProgress) are maintained only in the current page session and are not encoded into URL parameters; reloading the page or navigating away and back will restore the last saved state as reported by getChecklistDetailWithProgress for the given 'id'. If the 'id' parameter is missing or invalid, the page enters an error state and does not attempt to display sections or items, instead prompting the user to navigate back to checklists.html.

components.functionality: Reads the 'id' URL parameter, calls getChecklistDetailWithProgress with that checklistId, and initializes or refreshes shared page state with the loaded Checklist, sections, items, and saved status. Handles loading and error states.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: When the page first loads, read the 'id' URL parameter; if present, invoke getChecklistDetailWithProgress(checklistId) and store the response (checklist, sections, last_saved_at, is_saved, and per-item is_completed) in a shared state accessible to other components.

onUrlParamChange: When the 'id' URL parameter changes while the page is active, re-invoke getChecklistDetailWithProgress with the new checklistId, replace the existing checklist-related state, reset unsaved-change indicators, and clear any previous error messages.
onLoadSuccess: After a successful response, propagate the checklist data, section list, item list with is_completed flags, and last_saved_at to the header, sections list, and save bar.

onLoadError: If getChecklistDetailWithProgress fails or returns invalid data, set an error state for the page (e.g., message that the checklist could not be loaded) and notify dependent components to disable interactions that require valid checklist data.

components.functionality: Provides a simple navigation element that allows the user to go back to the full checklist list page (checklists.html). Optionally shows the current checklist title for context.
event_handlers: onChecklistDataChange: When the Checklist is loaded or changes (due to a new 'id'), update the breadcrumb label (for example, 'Back to all checklists' with an optional suffix based on the checklist title).
onClick: When the breadcrumb or back link is clicked, navigate the browser to checklists.html without additional parameters.

components.functionality: Displays the checklist title, optional description, overall completion summary (e.g., completed vs. total items), and the saved status including last saved time. It also reflects whether there are unsaved changes based on current item state vs. the last saved state.
event_handlers: onChecklistDataChange: When new checklist data is loaded, display the checklist title and description and reset the saved/unsaved indicators based on is_saved and last_saved_at from the initial load.

onItemCompletionStateChange: When any item’s checkbox is toggled in the sections list, recalculate the current completion summary (e.g., number of items marked complete and total items) and compare the current set of completedItemIds against the last known saved set to determine if there are unsaved changes.
onSaveResult: When saveChecklistProgress completes, update the last_saved_at timestamp and saved status using the returned checklist_progress, and clear the unsaved changes indicator.

components.functionality: Renders all ChecklistSections and their associated ChecklistItems in order. For each item, displays its text and a checkbox reflecting the current completion status (using is_completed from the loader and user interactions). Manages the in-page completion state of each item.
event_handlers: onChecklistDataChange: When new sections and items are loaded from getChecklistDetailWithProgress, render each section and its items in the specified order, and initialize each item’s checkbox state based on the is_completed flags in the response.

onItemToggle: When a user clicks an item’s checkbox, toggle that item’s completion state in page state; notify the header and save bar so that completion summaries and unsaved-change indicators can be updated.

onMarkSectionAll: If provided (e.g., an optional per-section control), when a user chooses to mark all items in a section as completed, set completion state to true for all items in that section and trigger updates to dependent components.
onMarkSectionNone: If provided, when a user chooses to clear all items in a section, set completion state to false for all items in that section and trigger updates to dependent components.

components.functionality: Provides the 'Save Checklist' button and related status messaging. Determines whether saving is necessary/enabled based on unsaved changes, calls saveChecklistProgress with the current list of completed item IDs, and updates saved-state indicators based on the response.
event_handlers: onItemCompletionStateChange: When any item’s completion state changes, recompute the current list of completedItemIds and mark the checklist as having unsaved changes; enable the Save button if there is at least one change since the last save or since initial load.

onClickSave: When the user clicks the Save button, gather the IDs of all items currently marked complete and call saveChecklistProgress(checklistId, completedItemIds). Set a transient 'saving' state while waiting for the response to prevent duplicate submissions.

onSaveSuccess: When saveChecklistProgress returns success = true, update the stored ChecklistProgress with the returned checklist_progress (including last_saved_at and completed_item_ids). Clear the unsaved changes flag, update any status text (e.g., last saved time), and notify the header component of the new saved state.

onSaveError: When saveChecklistProgress returns success = false or fails, display the message returned by the interface as an error notification and keep the unsaved changes flag active so the user can attempt to save again.

components.functionality: Shows functional error or empty states related to loading or saving the checklist, such as missing/invalid 'id' parameter or failed data load. Provides a way for the user to recover by navigating back to checklists.html.
event_handlers: onLoadError: When the data loader reports an error (missing or invalid checklistId, or failed getChecklistDetailWithProgress call), display an error message and disable the section list and save bar interactions.

onMissingIdDetected: If no 'id' URL parameter is present when the page loads, immediately display a message indicating that no checklist could be identified and prompt the user to go back to the checklists list.
onClickBackToChecklists: When the user clicks the provided recovery link or button, navigate the browser to checklists.html.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered max-width container (approx. 1100–1200px). Main content column spans roughly 8–9 columns and the sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The checklist detail page uses the same centered, max-width (≈1100–1200px) container and implicit 12‑column grid as the mockup. Inside this container, the main area is split into a dominant left column (≈8–9 columns / 70% width) for the checklist content and a secondary right column (≈3–4 columns / 30% width) reserved for global sidebar content. All provided checklist components stack in a clear, linear top‑to‑bottom order within the left column: breadcrumb at the top, then the checklist header, then any error message, followed by the scrollable checklist sections, with the save bar anchored to the bottom. Within the checklist itself, each item is arranged as a horizontal media-object row, with a checkbox on the left and text on the right, mirroring the image‑plus‑text pattern from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The checklist detail page uses the same centered, max-width (≈1100–1200px) container and implicit 12‑column grid as the mockup. Inside this container, the main area is split into a dominant left column (≈8–9 columns / 70% width) for the checklist content and a secondary right column (≈3–4 columns / 30% width) reserved for global sidebar content. All provided checklist components stack in a clear, linear top‑to‑bottom order within the left column: breadcrumb at the top, then the checklist header, then any error message, followed by the scrollable checklist sections, with the save bar anchored to the bottom. Within the checklist itself, each item is arranged as a horizontal media-object row, with a checkbox on the left and text on the right, mirroring the image‑plus‑text pattern from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The vertical header band of the site remains shallow (≈10–15% of initial viewport height), leaving most vertical space for the checklist. Below it, the main content container is padded horizontally (≈24–32px from the viewport edges) and centered. Within this, the left checklist column occupies about 70% of the width, while the right sidebar takes 30%. The breadcrumb and header use only the left column width; the error state and checklist sections also live entirely in that column. The save bar spans the full width of the central container to visually anchor the page. Vertical spacing follows the spacing scale: 24–32px between major blocks (breadcrumb → header → error → checklist list) and 16–24px between sub‑elements like section titles and item rows.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a moderately spacious editorial feel: ample white space around the breadcrumb, a generously padded header block, and clear separation between checklist sections. Each item row shows a checkbox, label, and any small meta text without crowding. Sections are visually grouped with 24–32px vertical gaps, preventing the list from feeling dense even when long. The save bar is compact but not cramped, using around 16px vertical padding. Overall density prioritizes readability and quick scanning over maximum item-per-screen count.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F-pattern adapted to the checklist context. After the global site header, the eye lands on the breadcrumb at the upper left, then drops slightly to the larger checklist header (title and status). From there, the eye moves down into the main checklist sections, scanning each row left‑to‑right from checkbox to text, similar to image‑to‑title flow in the mockup. The error message, when present, sits just under the header and is easily noticed before users interact with items. As the user scrolls, the sticky save bar at the bottom edge of the viewport provides a consistent horizontal anchor, drawing occasional attention to progress and actions. The secondary right sidebar content remains peripheral, seen after the primary checklist flow is established.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the site’s shallow top header, a centered, white page body sits within a broad max-width container, framed by comfortable side gutters. The main area is split into a wider left column and a narrower right column, echoing a classic blog layout. At the top of the left column, a subtle breadcrumb line leads into a more prominent checklist header band that introduces the checklist title and completion status. Directly below, any error or warning message appears as a full-width strip within the left column, then the layout opens into vertically stacked checklist sections. Each section reads like an editorial block: a clear section heading, followed by evenly spaced rows where a checkbox on the left aligns with descriptive text on the right, forming a tidy series of horizontal media-object rows down the page. Generous vertical spacing prevents visual overload while maintaining a strong rhythm. As the user scrolls through the sections, a slim, flat save bar remains pinned along the bottom of the central content, spanning the same width as the main container and housing the primary save action on the right. The right sidebar, though present, feels secondary, allowing the eye to naturally travel from top-left breadcrumb through header and list items, down to the persistent save controls, within a clean, marine‑accented, highly readable interface.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as a lightweight overlay within the central page container, activating over both the left checklist column and the right sidebar, but stopping at the same max-width boundaries so it feels contained rather than full-bleed. When active, it dims the underlying content slightly with a semi‑transparent white veil while keeping the overall page structure visible, echoing the flat, minimal style. At the visual center of the container (horizontally centered between left and right columns, and vertically centered within the visible viewport area below the site header), a simple spinner or loading indicator appears, roughly 48–64px in size, with a short status label beneath it. The overlay begins just below the global header and extends down to the bottom edge of the viewport, making it clear that interaction is blocked while preserving the sense of the underlying layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a full-height overlay within the main content region, positioned fixed relative to the viewport but constrained to the max-width central container; covers both columns while visible and scroll is effectively disabled for the underlying content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation sits at the top-left of the main content area, directly under the global header and aligned with the left edge of the left column. It spans only the width of that left column (≈70% of the container), keeping a clear gutter to the right sidebar. The breadcrumb appears as a single, unobtrusive horizontal line of text, about one line tall, with 24–32px of top margin from the bottom of the site header and 8–16px of bottom margin before the checklist header. It is left-aligned, using a small, low-contrast type treatment to be readable but visually secondary. The surrounding white space reinforces the editorial feel and sets up the vertical rhythm for the rest of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the left column; scrolls away naturally with the page and does not stick or overlay other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checklist header occupies a prominent horizontal band immediately below the breadcrumb, extending the full width of the left column (≈8–9 grid columns). It is visually similar in weight to a post header in the mockup: a bold checklist title on the left and concise status information on the right (e.g., items completed or last saved). The band is roughly 80–120px tall, with 16–24px padding on all sides inside the header block. The title is left-aligned, occupying roughly the left two-thirds of the band, while the status text or pills align to the right third, forming a balanced horizontal composition. There is 24–32px of white space below this header before the next element (error state or checklist list), creating a clear separation that signals a transition from overview to actionable content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component anchored under the breadcrumb within the left column; scrolls with the page and sets the top of the main content rhythm but does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state displays as a full-width message strip within the left column, positioned directly below the checklist header and above the checklist sections. It spans the entire width of the left column with 16–24px internal padding and rounded corners or a subtle outline, consistent with the flat design. The height is determined by its content, typically 48–72px for a single-line message and icon. A small warning or info icon sits on the left, with the error text to its right, both left-aligned so they align with the header’s left edge. There is about 16px of vertical space above and below the error block, so it distinctly separates the header from the section list without feeling cramped. When absent, the checklist section list moves up to sit just beneath the header, maintaining the same vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the left column that appears and disappears based on error conditions; it pushes content below it downward and scrolls away naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checklist section list fills the remainder of the left column beneath the header (and any error message), forming the main scrollable content area. It aligns flush with the left edge of the header and breadcrumb and spans the full width of the left column up to the gutter that separates it from the right sidebar. Sections are stacked vertically with 24–32px of space between them. Each section begins with a section heading row (title, optional description) followed by item rows. Item rows follow a media-object pattern: a checkbox or status icon on the far left (taking about 10–15% of the row’s width), with the item label, and any supporting detail text to the right (occupying the remaining 85–90%). Rows are left-aligned, with about 12–16px horizontal spacing between the checkbox and text, and 8–16px vertical spacing between item rows inside a section. The entire list can extend well below the initial viewport, with the user scrolling down through this column while the right sidebar scrolls in parallel. There is 32–48px of padding at the bottom of the list to ensure it does not visually collide with the save bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies a flexible vertical space in the left column, expanding as needed; scrolls beneath the fixed save bar, with sufficient bottom padding so the last items remain fully visible above the bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save bar appears as a slim, horizontally oriented action bar pinned to the bottom of the viewport within the central max-width container. It spans across both the left checklist column and the right sidebar, matching the width of the main content container so it reads as a page-level control. The bar is approximately 56–64px tall with 16px of internal vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding on each side. Inside, content is arranged in a simple left-to-right layout: progress or status text on the left (e.g., “12 of 24 items complete”), and the primary action button on the far right, styled in the bright marine-blue accent color. On wide screens, the left status occupies about half the bar, while the right half is reserved for the button and any secondary link. The bar sits flush with the bottom of the viewport, with a subtle top border line to distinguish it from the checklist content above, without any heavy shadows. The checklist content above scrolls behind it, but generous bottom padding in the checklist list ensures no items are hidden behind the bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky to the bottom of the viewport within the page container (positioned fixed or sticky relative to the viewport), always visible as the user scrolls; spans the full width of the central container and remains aligned with it on all screen sizes.

name: Tools
title: Tools
description: Lists available boating tools and utilities, surfaces recommended tools for beginners and safety planning, and routes users to individual tool pages with contextual safety help.

core_features: Load all available tools using the listTools interface and render them as selectable tool entries.
Allow navigation from each tool entry to its dedicated tool page (e.g., boat capacity calculator, engine maintenance scheduler) using the tool slug in the URL.
Highlight a specific tool based on the optional highlight_tool URL parameter and make it easier to notice and access.
Surface a subset of tools as recommended for beginners and safety planning, using the Tool data (e.g., is_featured and description) returned by listTools.
Provide contextual help per tool explaining how the tool supports safe boating decisions, combining the tool’s description with generic safety guidance.
Allow users to adjust which tools are shown (all vs. recommended) via a simple view toggle that is persisted in URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Browse all tools from navigation:
1. User clicks the "Tools" link in the site header.
2. tools.html loads and reads URL parameters (highlight_tool and optional tools_view).
3. The page controller calls listTools with no parameters.
4. When listTools returns, the page computes derived subsets (e.g., recommended tools using Tool.is_featured and safety-related signals in Tool.description).
5. All tools are displayed in the main tools list; a recommended tools area is populated if any recommended tools exist.
6. User scrolls the list, opens contextual help for specific tools as needed, and clicks a tool entry to navigate to that tool’s dedicated page (using the tool.slug to build the URL).

Arrive from Home with a highlighted tool (e.g., a safety-related tool):
1. User clicks a link on index.html that points to tools.html with a highlight_tool URL parameter (e.g., tools.html?highlight_tool=boat_capacity_calculator).
2. tools.html reads highlight_tool from the URL and stores it in in-memory page state.
3. The page controller calls listTools.
4. After tools are loaded, the page finds the tool whose slug matches highlight_tool.
5. That tool is visually emphasized in the main list and, if configured, its contextual help is automatically expanded and scrolled into view.
6. User reviews the contextual safety explanation, then clicks the highlighted tool to navigate to the tool’s dedicated page.

Focus only on recommended tools for beginners and safety planning:
1. User arrives on tools.html (with or without highlight_tool) and the page calls listTools.
2. After tools are loaded, the page computes which tools are recommended (e.g., Tool.is_featured is true and/or Tool.description indicates beginner friendliness or safety planning relevance).
3. User toggles the view mode to show only recommended tools (e.g., via a "View" selector).
4. The page updates the tools_view URL parameter (e.g., tools_view=recommended) and re-filters the in-memory list without additional API calls.
5. Only recommended tools are displayed; any active highlight_tool is still applied within this filtered set.
6. User opens contextual help for one or more recommended tools, then clicks a tool entry to navigate to its dedicated page.

Use contextual help to understand how a tool supports safe boating decisions:
1. User is on tools.html and sees a list of tools loaded from listTools.
2. For a tool of interest, user clicks a "Learn how this helps" or similar interaction.
3. The page opens a contextual help panel or inline expansion bound to that specific Tool record.
4. The panel shows the Tool.description along with generic safety framing (e.g., what types of decisions the tool can inform), built dynamically without hardcoding tool-specific text.
5. User closes the help panel or moves to another tool’s help, then optionally clicks through to the tool’s dedicated page.
interactions:
On page load (tools.html):
- Read URL parameters: highlight_tool (string) and optional tools_view (string, e.g., 'all' or 'recommended').
- Initialize page state with these values.
- Call listTools() to retrieve the current list of Tool entities.
- When listTools resolves, store the tools in state, compute recommended subsets, and apply any highlight_tool and tools_view logic before the initial render.

Click on a tool entry (card or list item):
- Event handler receives the clicked Tool object.
- Build a navigation URL for the tool’s dedicated page using the tool.slug (e.g., `${tool.slug}.html`).
- Optionally preserve the current tools_view or highlight_tool in the outgoing URL if needed by the target page (not required by this specification but allowed by the URL-parameter approach).
- Trigger browser navigation to the constructed URL.

Toggle tools view mode (all vs. recommended):
- User selects a view mode (e.g., clicking a toggle or selecting from a dropdown).
- Event handler updates in-memory state for currentView (e.g., 'all' or 'recommended').
- Event handler updates the tools_view URL parameter to match the selected view (e.g., tools_view=recommended) without reloading the page, so the state is shareable/bookmarkable.
- Render logic filters the in-memory Tool list based on currentView (no additional API calls).

Open contextual help for a tool:
- User clicks a help affordance associated with a Tool entry.
- Event handler sets the activeHelpToolId in state to the id of the selected Tool (or toggles it off if the same tool is clicked again).
- The contextual help component renders a panel or inline section using the Tool data (e.g., Tool.name and Tool.description), and wraps it with generic guidance on how to use such a tool for safe boating decisions.
- The page may optionally adjust scroll position to bring the contextual help into view for accessibility.

Apply highlight_tool behavior:
- After tools are loaded from listTools, the page compares highlight_tool (from URL) against each Tool.slug.
- If a match is found, that tool is marked in state as highlighted (e.g., highlightedToolId).
- The list component uses highlightedToolId to visually emphasize that tool and optionally auto-expand its contextual help.
- If tools_view=recommended is active and the highlighted tool is not in the recommended subset, the page may either (a) force-include the highlighted tool in the filtered view or (b) show a non-blocking notice that the highlighted tool is outside the current filter (implementation detail controlled by state logic, not static data).

Handle URL changes (e.g., user manually edits parameters or uses history navigation):
- On popstate or equivalent event, re-read highlight_tool and tools_view from the URL.
- Update the in-memory state accordingly (currentView, highlightedToolSlug).
- Re-run filtering/highlighting logic on the already-loaded tools without re-calling listTools, unless a full page reload occurs.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses only URL parameters and in-memory state (no localStorage) to manage and persist user-facing state. On initial load, tools.html reads two parameters: (1) highlight_tool: an optional Tool.slug string that indicates which tool should be visually highlighted and potentially auto-expanded in the contextual help; and (2) tools_view: an optional string representing the current view mode (e.g., 'all' or 'recommended'). If tools_view is absent, the default in-memory state is 'all'. After reading these values, the page calls listTools() once to fetch all Tool entities. When listTools resolves, the tools are stored in memory, and derived subsets are computed: a recommended subset based on Tool.is_featured and any safety-relevant signals in Tool.description; a highlighted tool derived by matching Tool.slug to highlight_tool. Rendering logic is then driven solely by this in-memory state and URL parameters: if tools_view='recommended', only recommended tools (plus any forced-in highlighted tool, if the implementation chooses that behavior) are shown; otherwise all tools are shown. If the user changes view mode, the tools_view parameter in the URL is updated via the History API to reflect the new state, and the list is re-filtered without new API calls. If the user navigates via the browser’s back/forward buttons or manually edits the URL parameters, a URL-change listener re-reads highlight_tool and tools_view, updates state, and causes the UI to re-render accordingly. No state is persisted beyond what is encoded in the URL; a full page reload simply re-applies the same rules by re-reading the URL and re-calling listTools().

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading via listTools, manages URL-parameter state (highlight_tool and tools_view), maintains in-memory collections of all tools and recommended tools, and passes filtered data and state flags down to child components.
Allows the user to switch between viewing all tools and viewing only recommended tools. Updates the tools_view URL parameter and notifies the page controller of the selected mode.

Displays a concise list or strip of tools that are computed as recommended for beginners and safety planning, based on Tool.is_featured and other dynamic attributes from listTools. Provides quick access to these tools and their contextual help.

Renders the primary list of tools based on the current view mode and highlight state. Supports visual emphasis of the highlighted tool (derived from highlight_tool) and forwards tool interactions (selection and help toggling) to the controller.

Represents a single tool in either the recommended strip or main list. Binds directly to one Tool entity, displays its key metadata (e.g., Tool.name and Tool.description summary), visually indicates if it is highlighted or recommended, and exposes actions for opening the tool’s page and toggling contextual help.

Shows contextual information for a selected Tool, explaining how it supports safe boating decisions. It uses the Tool.description as the primary content and augments it with generic text about decision support and safety planning, without hardcoding any tool-specific data. Opens and closes in response to help toggle events.

Interprets the highlight_tool URL parameter and manages which Tool is considered highlighted. After tools are loaded from listTools, it matches Tool.slug to highlight_tool, sets highlightedToolId in state, and informs list components so they can emphasize the matching tool and optionally auto-expand its contextual help.

Constructs and triggers navigation URLs for individual tools when a tool is selected. Uses the Tool.slug to build the target URL (e.g., `${tool.slug}.html`) without hardcoding tool names or descriptions, and initiates browser navigation upon user click.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered max-width container (approx. 1100–1200px). Main content column spans roughly 8–9 columns and the sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s classic blog layout: a centered max-width container on a 12-column grid with a dominant left column for the main tool content and a narrower right sidebar for contextual help. Within the main column, content flows top-to-bottom in this order: page heading, a subtle view-toggle/filter bar, a short block of recommended tools, then the full tools list. Each tool in the lists is presented as a media-object row: thumbnail on the left, text and primary CTA on the right. The sidebar begins at the same vertical level as the recommended tools block and stacks its widgets vertically, starting with the context help panel. All elements sit in a linear document flow; no overlapping layers or floating cards.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s classic blog layout: a centered max-width container on a 12-column grid with a dominant left column for the main tool content and a narrower right sidebar for contextual help. Within the main column, content flows top-to-bottom in this order: page heading, a subtle view-toggle/filter bar, a short block of recommended tools, then the full tools list. Each tool in the lists is presented as a media-object row: thumbnail on the left, text and primary CTA on the right. The sidebar begins at the same vertical level as the recommended tools block and stacks its widgets vertically, starting with the context help panel. All elements sit in a linear document flow; no overlapping layers or floating cards.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies a shallow band at the top (roughly 10–15% of the initial viewport height), leaving the rest for the tools content. The main container is centered and limited to about 1100–1200px wide, with generous left and right padding (24–48px) for whitespace on large screens. Inside the container, the main tools column uses about 70% of the width (8–9 grid columns) and the sidebar about 30% (3–4 columns), separated by a clear gutter (24–32px). Each tool row allocates roughly 30–35% of its width to the thumbnail on the left and 65–70% to the textual content and CTA on the right. Vertical gaps between major sections (heading to view toggle, view toggle to recommended strip, recommended strip to main list, and between sidebar widgets) are in the 32–48px range, echoing the editorial spaciousness from the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content density is moderately spacious. Each tool row has a single image, a concise title, a brief descriptor/meta line, a short explanatory excerpt, and one prominent CTA button. There is no stacking of multiple controls or dense metadata. Recommended tools are presented as a small, clearly separated block rather than a crowded grid. The sidebar’s context help panel has generous padding and clear typographic hierarchy with plenty of breathing room around text. Overall, white space is used deliberately to support scannability and reduce cognitive load.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the F-pattern from the mockup: after the global header, the eye lands on the left-aligned page title in the main column, then moves slightly down to the view-toggle bar. From there, scanning continues down the left column through the recommended tools block and then the main tools list, moving left-to-right within each row from thumbnail to title, then down to the excerpt and finally to the marine-blue CTA button. After a few rows, the eye is drawn to the right-hand sidebar by the contrasting label bar at the top of the context help panel, then flows downward through any additional sidebar content. Highlight states on tool rows subtly reinforce this flow by visually connecting selected tools on the left with their contextual explanation on the right.

layout.overall_description: The Tools page sits within the same clean, minimalist frame as the rest of the boating blog. A shallow global header with the centered boat logo spans the top, beneath which a spacious white band introduces the content. Within a centered, max-width container, the layout splits into two columns: a broad left column for the tools themselves and a narrower right sidebar for safety-focused context. At the top of the left column, the page title and a slim view-toggle bar establish control and orientation. Just below, a lightly accented block surfaces a short list of recommended tools for beginners. Beneath this, the main tools list runs vertically as a sequence of consistent media-object rows—each with a flat thumbnail on the left and a clear title, brief explanation, and marine-blue action button on the right. The right-hand sidebar starts with a strong dark-blue label bar that caps the tool context help panel, visually echoing the “ABOUT THE AUTHOR” card from the mockup. This panel stays visible as users scroll, updating its content in sync with whichever tool row is highlighted on the left. Generous vertical spacing and a restrained color palette keep the page calm and readable, while the left-to-right, top-to-bottom flow makes it easy for visitors to move from discovering tools to understanding their safety implications.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools-page-controller defines the overall page canvas beneath the global site header. It creates a centered content container with a max width of approximately 1100–1200px and horizontal padding of 24–32px on both sides, ensuring generous margins on large screens and aligning with the mockup’s editorial feel. Vertically, there is about 48px of space between the bottom of the global header and the start of the tools content, giving the page title and top controls room to breathe. Inside this container, the controller establishes a two-column layout on a 12-column grid: the left main column spans roughly 8–9 columns (around 70% width) and the right sidebar spans 3–4 columns (around 30% width), with a 24–32px gutter between them. All child components—page heading, tools-view-toggle, recommended-tools-strip, tools-main-list in the left column, and tool-context-help-panel in the right column—are laid out in a vertical flow within their respective columns, honoring the consistent vertical rhythm of 24–48px spacing between sections. On narrower screens (tablet and below), the controller collapses the grid into a single column, stacking the sidebar (context help panel) below the main tools list while preserving the same vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Structural flow container; not fixed or floating. It controls the grid behavior, including responsive shift from a two-column layout on desktop to a stacked single-column layout on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools-view-toggle sits at the top of the left column, directly beneath the page title text (which is managed outside this component). It is aligned flush with the left edge of the main column content and spans about 60–70% of the main column’s width, leaving some white space on the right to keep it visually light. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space between the page title and the top edge of this toggle bar, and around 24–32px of space between its bottom edge and the start of the recommended-tools-strip. The component itself appears as a low-profile horizontal bar: a light gray bottom border stretches the full width of the component, visually anchoring it without adding weight. Within the bar, control elements (e.g., segmented view switch, simple filters) are left-aligned, with 16–24px horizontal padding from the left edge and 8–12px vertical padding, following the component padding guidelines. The right side of the bar remains mostly empty or may contain a subtle text label, maintaining the mockup’s minimal, breathable feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in the normal document flow at the top of the main column. It scrolls away with the content and does not remain fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recommended-tools-strip occupies the full width of the left main column, immediately below the tools-view-toggle. It is separated from the toggle by roughly 24–32px of vertical space to signal a new content block. The strip is styled as a subtle section within the column, with a very light tinted background (e.g., a soft gray or the faintest marine-blue wash) and a thin, light border or top accent line to distinguish it from the main list without adding visual heaviness. Inside this strip, there is 24px of padding on all sides. At the top-left of the strip, a small, bolded section label such as “Recommended for new boaters” or similar is placed, aligning with the main column’s left edge. Below this label, recommended tools are presented using standard tool-card instances, stacked vertically in 1–3 rows depending on content. Each recommended tool-card follows the same media-object layout as cards in the main list but may use a slightly higher visual emphasis—such as a subtle blue dot or tiny tag near the title—without changing its structure. Vertical spacing of 16–24px separates each card within the strip. The bottom of the strip is separated from the tools-main-list below by around 32–40px, aligning with the mockup’s clear section separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives within the main column’s normal flow. On smaller screens, it remains above the main tools list, maintaining its padding and background distinction while expanding to full width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools-main-list sits directly below the recommended-tools-strip in the left main column, separated by about 32–40px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the main column, aligning its left edge with the other primary left-column content. The list renders as a vertical stack of tool-card components, each occupying a single row. Each tool-card extends nearly the full width of the main column, with consistent horizontal padding inside the cards themselves, while the list maintains 24–32px of vertical spacing between each card to preserve the airy editorial feel. There are no enclosing heavy borders around the entire list; instead, individual cards are visually separated by their own subtle borders and the white space between them. As users scroll, this list provides the primary vertical rhythm of the page, mirroring the mockup’s blog-post index: image and title combinations repeat predictably, encouraging scanning down the left side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard vertical list in the normal flow. It scrolls beneath the global header and alongside the sidebar; cards within the list adjust responsively, stacking image above text on very narrow screens while preserving the same order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each tool-card is laid out as a horizontal media-object row within either the recommended-tools-strip or tools-main-list. The card has a white background, a very light gray 1px border, and optional subtle rounded corners (4–6px) to keep it visually soft and in line with the flat, trustworthy look. Inside the card, there is 16–24px of padding on all sides. The card is divided into two main regions: a left thumbnail area occupying approximately 30–35% of the card’s width and a right content area occupying 65–70%. The left side holds a rectangular, flat thumbnail image or icon with a fixed aspect ratio (e.g., 4:3), vertically centered relative to the content on the right; there is at least 8–12px of padding between the image and the card’s border. On the right side, text content is aligned to the left and follows a clear hierarchy: at the top, the tool name is presented in a bold sans-serif, larger than body text, with about 4–8px of space below. Directly under the title, a single line of muted metadata (e.g., “Beginner · 5–10 min”) appears in a smaller, lighter gray type. Beneath this, a 2–3 line excerpt explains what the tool does and why it matters for boating safety, with 8–12px of spacing above and below. At the bottom-left of the content area, aligned with the left edge of the text, sits the primary CTA button (e.g., “OPEN TOOL” or “LAUNCH TOOL”) in the bright marine-blue accent. The button uses 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, with a flat, no-shadow appearance. The overall card height is determined by the text content but is generally consistent across cards to create a regular rhythm. On hover or active selection, the card’s border changes to a 2px marine-blue outline and the background lightens slightly, communicating the highlight-manager state without altering the card’s position or size.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a block-level element within its list, filling the available width of the column. On narrower viewports, the layout can reflow to stack the thumbnail above the text: the image sits on top, full-width, with text and the CTA below, while maintaining similar internal padding and vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tool-context-help-panel occupies the top of the right sidebar column, aligned horizontally with the recommended-tools-strip in the main column. It spans the full width of the sidebar (roughly 30% of the overall content width) and is separated from the top of the content area by the same vertical offset as the main column, ensuring the two columns start on a visually aligned baseline. The panel is styled as a prominent card, echoing the mockup’s “ABOUT THE AUTHOR” widget. At its very top, a horizontal label bar runs edge-to-edge across the card, approximately 40–48px tall, in a dark marine-blue fill with white, uppercase text such as “SAFETY CONTEXT” or “TOOL HELP.” Below this label bar, the main body of the panel has a white background with 24px of internal padding. Content inside is vertically stacked: an optional centered circular icon or small illustration at the top (occupying the top 64–80px), followed by a bold subheading (e.g., the currently highlighted tool’s name or safety category) aligned left, and then a few short paragraphs or bullet points describing safety considerations, prerequisites, or best practices for the selected tool. Line spacing is generous to maintain readability. The bottom of the panel has at least 16–24px of extra padding to give the content a comfortable end. If additional sidebar widgets are added later, they are stacked beneath this panel with 32–40px of vertical spacing. The panel’s presence and dark label bar draw the user’s eye to the right after scanning the left-column tools.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and large tablets, the panel is sticky within the viewport: as the user scrolls the main tools list, this card remains pinned near the top of the sidebar, updating its content when different tool-cards become active. On smaller screens where the layout collapses to a single column, it loses its sticky behavior and scrolls normally, appearing either above or below the main list depending on the responsive strategy, while maintaining its internal padding and top label bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlight-manager does not render as a visible standalone block but affects the visual treatment of other components, primarily tool-card and tool-context-help-panel. Spatially, it manifests as consistent highlight styles layered on top of the existing layout without changing structure. When a tool-card is hovered or selected, the highlight-manager applies a 2px marine-blue border around the card and a very subtle background tint within the card’s existing bounds. It also ensures there is always at least 8–12px of space between the highlighted border and adjacent cards, preserving the perceived white-space rhythm. At any given time, only one tool-card in the visible list appears in the ‘active’ state, preventing the layout from looking cluttered.
