When the user submits the form and validation passes, the state transitions to 'submittingForm' and submitContactForm is called with the current form values; on response, if success is true, the state transitions to 'submitSuccess' and stores ticket_id and response.message; if success is false, the state transitions to 'submitError' while keeping form values intact. URL parameters are not mutated by the page; the 'topic' parameter reflects only how the user arrived at contact.html. To change the topic stored in the URL, the user would need to navigate via a link that encodes a different topic; the form's topic field changes do not update the URL. After submission, the URL remains contact.html (with any existing topic parameter), and the success or error status is reflected only in internal UI state, not in the URL. Navigation to index.html via the 'Return to Home' link loads the home page without additional parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes the Contact page by calling getContactPageContent to retrieve title, intro_text, contact_email, mailing_address, and response_expectations_text, and exposes this data to child components while managing loading and error states.

Reads the 'topic' URL parameter from the current page URL and provides its value as the initial topic for the contact form; if no parameter is present, it provides an empty or undefined topic. It can also react to full page reloads with different topic parameters.

Displays non-form contact information retrieved from getContactPageContent, including intro_text, contact_email, mailing_address, and response_expectations_text, so users can see how to contact the site and what response timing to expect without submitting the form.

Provides a form with fields for name, email, topic, and message; initializes the topic field with the value supplied by contact-topic-initializer; validates user input; and submits the data using submitContactForm without a full page reload.

Listens for submission results from the contact-form component and displays the current status, including success or error messages. On success, it shows confirmation using the response.message and ticket_id returned by submitContactForm; on error, it exposes response.message and allows the user to retry.

When the contact-submission-status component indicates a successful submission, this component shows a navigational option to return to the home page (index.html) and an option to send another message by resetting the form and status state while keeping the current URL parameters unchanged.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered max-width container (approx. 1100–1200px). Main content column spans roughly 8–9 columns and the sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the two-column editorial layout from the mockup. The main left column is dedicated to the contact interaction (page title, short intro, contact form, and submission status), stacked vertically. The right sidebar follows the blog's sidebar pattern and is used for the contact-info panel (address, email, safety note, response times, etc.). Invisible logic components (loader and URL state initializer) affect behavior but not layout; the loader briefly overlays the main content area, while the topic initializer preconfigures the form fields. All content flows top-to-bottom, with clear vertical separation between the title/intro, the form, the status, and any post-submit navigation links.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the two-column editorial layout from the mockup. The main left column is dedicated to the contact interaction (page title, short intro, contact form, and submission status), stacked vertically. The right sidebar follows the blog's sidebar pattern and is used for the contact-info panel (address, email, safety note, response times, etc.). Invisible logic components (loader and URL state initializer) affect behavior but not layout; the loader briefly overlays the main content area, while the topic initializer preconfigures the form fields. All content flows top-to-bottom, with clear vertical separation between the title/intro, the form, the status, and any post-submit navigation links.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The entire contact page content sits inside a centered max-width container of about 1100–1200px, with generous horizontal margins on large screens. Within this container, the main contact column occupies roughly 70% of the width (about 8–9 grid columns) on the left, and the sidebar takes the remaining 30% (about 3–4 columns) on the right. The header above remains relatively shallow (10–15% viewport height) so most vertical space is reserved for the form and info panel. Vertically, large section margins (around 48px) separate the header from the main content, the main title/intro from the form, and the form from status/navigation. Within cards and blocks (form, info panel), internal padding is about 16–24px, with 24–32px vertical spacing between field groups and text sections.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page follows the mockup’s moderately spacious editorial tone. The form fields are stacked in a single column with comfortable vertical spacing (around 16–24px between fields), clear labels, and ample whitespace so the page never feels cramped. The contact-info panel uses generous padding, a large readable type scale, and avoids clutter—only essential contact details, each separated by 8–16px. There are no dense clusters of buttons or metadata; instead, there is a single primary submit CTA in the form and a small set of lightly styled navigation links after submission. The overall density is tuned for calm, legible reading and confident form completion rather than maximum information per screen.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the F-pattern from the mockup. After the site header, the eye is drawn to a bold 'Contact' or 'Get in touch' heading near the top-left of the main content. From there, the user scans left-to-right across a short intro line, then moves down into the vertical stack of form fields, ending at a bright marine-blue submit button. After interacting with the form, the status message appears in the same left column just above or below the submit area, becoming a momentary focal point. As users finish scanning the form, their attention naturally moves right toward the sidebar, where the contact-info panel echoes the 'About the author' card with a dark label bar and clear structure. The post-submit navigation links appear directly below the main form/status area, inviting the eye to move slightly downward or across, keeping the experience linear and predictable.

layout.overall_description: The contact page sits within the same clean, minimalist blog framework as the rest of the site. A shallow header with the centered boat logo, left-aligned MENU label, and right-aligned search icon spans the top. Below this, after a generous vertical gap, a two-column layout fills a centered content container. On the left, a wide main column holds a bold 'Contact' heading and brief explanatory text, followed by a single, card-like contact form that runs down the page in one clear column, ending with a marine-blue submit button. On the right, a narrower sidebar contains a compact, card-style contact-info panel that mirrors the blog’s 'About the author' card, with a dark label bar at the top and organized contact details beneath. Whitespace between sections and around each card creates a calm, editorial feel. When the page is loading or a message is sending, a subtle overlay centered over the main contact area shows a simple loader, while URL-based topic initialization quietly configures the form without adding extra visual elements. On successful or failed submission, a status message appears in the main column near the bottom of the form, with understated navigation links just below, helping users move on to posts, FAQs, or home. The whole composition reads in an F-shaped pattern and feels airy, precise, and trustworthy, aligned with a safety-focused boating blog.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears only when the contact page or form state is in a loading or submitting phase. Visually, it is presented as a lightweight overlay confined to the main content area (not covering the global header). The overlay spans the full width of the main content container but is visually anchored over the left column, where the form resides, slightly dimming that area with a semi-transparent white layer. At the center of this left column, a small, minimalist spinner or progress indicator sits, with optional concise text such as 'Loading contact details…' or 'Sending your message…' beneath it. The overlay respects the container’s max-width and sits about one base spacing unit (8–16px) inset from the top of the main content area, so it feels integrated rather than full-screen. The sidebar stays visible but appears slightly de-emphasized behind the overlay, reinforcing that the primary action in progress is in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a temporary, centered overlay within the main content container. It is positioned above the normal stacking order of the main column (contact-form and contact-submission-status) and lightly over the sidebar, using fixed positioning relative to the container so it remains centered even if the user scrolls during the loading state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL-state router operates entirely behind the scenes to preselect the contact topic or subject inside the form and does not occupy its own visual space. Its effect is visible as certain form fields (for example, a 'Topic' dropdown or 'Regarding' field) appearing pre-filled or pre-focused when the page renders. Any visual manifestation is embedded within the contact-form component itself; there is no separate panel, text, or area dedicated to this logic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavioral component with no independent spatial footprint. It influences initial values and focus inside the contact-form but does not alter the page’s structural layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info panel is placed in the right-hand sidebar, occupying roughly 30% of the page width within the centered container, mirroring the visual weight and position of the 'About the author' card from the mockup. It begins near the top of the sidebar, vertically aligned with the top of the main column’s heading, with about 48px of vertical space separating it from the header area above. The panel itself is a rectangular card with a white background, subtle border or hairline divider, and 16–24px internal padding on all sides. At the very top of the card, a full-width horizontal label bar (about 40–48px tall) spans edge to edge within the panel, filled with a darker marine-blue or charcoal tone, containing an all-caps title such as 'CONTACT INFO' aligned left or centered. Below the bar, the panel content is stacked vertically: key contact details (email, phone, response time, and perhaps a short safety or support note) each presented on their own lines or small clusters, with 8–16px vertical spacing between items for clarity. Any small icons are aligned to the left of text and kept flat and simple. The panel does not extend the full height of the page; it ends after its content, leaving about 32–48px of whitespace below before any additional sidebar elements (if present).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the sidebar. It scrolls normally with the page content and does not stick or float; its height is determined entirely by its content, preserving the clean vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact form is the dominant element in the left column, occupying roughly 70% of the content width and sitting directly under the page heading and intro. The left column itself is aligned flush with the container’s left edge, starting about 48px below the site header. At the top of the form area, a bold, left-aligned heading like 'Get in touch' or 'Send us a message' sits with 8–16px space below it, followed by a short one- or two-line explanatory paragraph set in normal-weight body text. After another 24–32px gap, the form card begins. It uses a white background and subtle delineation from the page (a fine border or very light divider), with about 24px of padding on all sides. Inside, form fields are arranged in a single vertical column for maximal clarity: label above each input, then the input control, with 16–24px vertical spacing between fields. Where logical, two shorter fields (e.g., first name and last name) may be placed side-by-side within the same row, each occupying about 48–50% of the form’s internal width, reflecting a looser use of the implicit grid but preserving the overall left-aligned structure. The primary submit button is placed near the bottom-right of the form’s content area but still within the left column, set on its own line with about 24px of whitespace above it. This button uses the bright marine-blue accent color, full-width on small screens but on desktop occupying about one-third to one-half of the form width, and is vertically separated from the bottom of the card with about 16–24px padding. The overall height of the form card depends on the number of fields, but spacing remains generous to maintain the editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content within the main column. It participates in the normal document flow, expanding vertically as needed. On narrower viewports, it can responsively grow to occupy close to 100% width, with the sidebar stacking below, while preserving its internal vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission status display is positioned within the main left column, visually tied to the contact form so that feedback appears in context. By default, its space is either collapsed or reserved with a small vertical gap beneath the form’s heading. Once the user submits, a horizontal status bar or card appears just below the form’s title and intro and above the main body of form fields, or alternatively immediately below the submit button—both options keep it tightly associated with the action area. The status block spans nearly the full width of the form card (matching the internal padding and alignment of form fields), with 16–24px vertical padding and a subtle background tint: a very light green for success or light red/orange for errors, maintaining the minimalist look. Text inside is left-aligned, concise (one to three lines), and uses the same sans-serif body font. If there are validation errors, this block may include short bullet-like lines referencing the problematic fields but keeps the content sparse and scannable. There is about 16px of vertical space separating this status block from adjacent elements (the form header above or fields/button below) so it doesn’t feel cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically within the normal flow of the main column, pushing content down when visible rather than overlapping it. It does not float or stick; instead, it becomes part of the form’s vertical stack, preserving the consistent rhythm of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The post-submit navigation sits directly in the main left column below the contact form and its status display, acting as a gentle next-step area once the user’s message has been handled. Vertically, it begins about 32–48px below the bottom edge of the form card, maintaining strong separation between the interaction area and follow-up content. This navigation is a simple horizontal or stacked list of links, such as 'Back to articles,' 'Safety guides,' or 'FAQ,' aligned left and contained within the same width as the form, so the left edges line up. Each link is rendered as understated marine-blue text with sufficient line spacing (about 8–12px between items), optionally prefixed by small arrows or chevrons that remain flat and non-decorative. On wider screens, links may appear on a single line separated by vertical dividers or subtle dot separators; on narrower screens, they stack vertically to preserve readability. This block is compact and does not resemble a card; instead, it reads as a textual navigation row gently anchored at the bottom of the main column’s content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static navigation content that appears in the normal flow after submission. It does not fix or float and will scroll off-screen along with the rest of the main content. If no submission has occurred, it may be hidden or shown in a more neutral form, but it never changes the overall two-column layout.
